topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Components" in Co-operative Robotics

In the context of **Co-operative Robotics (PERUT861)**, the term **"Components"** refers to the integrated set of physical, computational, sensory, actuation, and communication subsystems that collectively enable a **Multi-Robot System (MRS)** to perceive, reason, act, and collaborate within a shared operational environment. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a co-operative robotic system is decomposed into five functional pillars:

1. **Robot Platform (Agent)** – The physical embodiment carrying sensors, actuators, and compute.
2. **Sensing Subsystem** – Proprioceptive and exteroceptive measurement devices.
3. **Actuation & Locomotion Subsystem** – Motors, drivers, and mechanical effectors.
4. **Communication Subsystem** – Wireless/wired channels enabling inter-agent data exchange.
5. **On-board Computation & Control Subsystem** – Embedded processors running perception, planning, and coordination algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A co-operative robotic system is not just a collection of robots; it is a **distributed cyber-physical system** where every individual component must be designed with **interoperability, scalability, and fault tolerance** in mind.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **flock of migratory birds** crossing the ocean:
- Each bird (the **agent/platform**) carries its own **eyes** (sensors), **wings** (actuators), and **tiny brain** (on-board computer).
- Birds communicate through **calls, body postures, and wing formations** (the **communication subsystem**).
- No single bird leads; instead, simple local rules (separation, alignment, cohesion) produce **emergent global intelligence** — this is the essence of co-operation.

In robotics, replace the bird with a **ground robot, drone, or manipulator arm**, the eyes with **LiDAR/cameras**, the wings with **BLDC motors**, the calls with **Wi-Fi/ROS 2 messages**, and the brain with an **embedded SBC (Single Board Computer)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Multi-Robot System (MRS):** A networked team of robots that share information and coordinate actions to achieve individual or collective goals that are difficult or impossible for a single robot.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics in Co-operative Robotics

- **Heterogeneity Coefficient (H):** A measure of how diverse the robot team is in terms of sensors and capabilities. **H = 0** indicates a **homogeneous** team (identical robots); **H = 1** indicates **fully heterogeneous** (maximally diverse).
- **Scalability Bound (N):** The maximum number of agents a coordination algorithm can support before performance degradation. Typical MRS research evaluates **N = 10 to 1000**.
- **Communication Range (R_c):** The maximum physical distance over which two agents can exchange data. Standard short-range values: **R_c ≈ 10–100 m** (Wi-Fi/802.11n); long-range: **R_c ≈ 1–10 km** (4G/5G).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Decomposition of a Co-operative Robot

Each robot agent in an MRS is built as a layered architecture. The standard breakdown adopted in KTU-aligned textbooks is:

### A. Mechanical Platform (Hardware Body)
- **Mobile bases:** Differential drive, Ackermann steering, omnidirectional (Mecanum/Omni-wheels), legged.
- **Aerial platforms:** Quadrotors, fixed-wing UAVs, hybrid VTOL.
- **Manipulators:** Serial (n-DOF arms), parallel (Stewart-Gough), soft grippers.
- **Marine/Underwater:** Thruster-based AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles).

### B. Sensors (Perception Layer)
Sensors are categorized into two families:

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Proprioceptive** | Measure internal state (self) | Encoders, IMU, gyroscope, battery monitor, current sensor |
| **Exteroceptive** | Measure external environment | LiDAR, RGB-D camera, ultrasonic, GPS, magnetometer, radar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two categories?** In a co-operative setting, proprioceptive data is **rarely shared** (each robot knows its own state) while exteroceptive data is the **primary payload of inter-robot communication**.

### C. Actuators & Drivers (Action Layer)
Common actuator types:
- **DC motors with gearbox** — simple, cheap, low precision.
- **BLDC (Brushless DC) motors** — high efficiency, used in drones and modern mobile robots.
- **Servo motors** — closed-loop position control, common in manipulators.
- **Stepper motors** — open-loop precision, useful in 3D printers and CNC-like platforms.
- **Pneumatic/Hydraulic** — high power-to-weight ratio, used in heavy manipulators.

### D. Communication Infrastructure
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)** — high bandwidth (~1 Gbps), short range.
- **Bluetooth / BLE** — low power, very short range (~10 m).
- **Zigbee / 802.15.4** — mesh networking, low data rate, ideal for swarms.
- **5G / LTE** — long range, low latency, infrastructure-dependent.
- **Acoustic / Optical** — used underwater or in EM-denied environments.

### E. On-board Computation
- **Microcontroller (MCU):** STM32, Arduino, ESP32 — real-time low-level control.
- **Single Board Computer (SBC):** Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson, Odroid — high-level perception and planning.
- **FPGA:** Xilinx/Intel — hardware-accelerated SLAM and vision pipelines.

## 2.2 The Five "C"s of Co-operative Robotics

The KTU Module 1 framework identifies five core capabilities that every co-operative robot component must support:

1. **Cognition** — Perceive, plan, decide.
2. **Communication** — Exchange state/intent.
3. **Coordination** — Align actions with teammates.
4. **Co-operation** — Joint task execution (e.g., payload transport).
5. **Continuity** — Sustain operation (battery, fault recovery).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $N_{links} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Total communication links in a **fully connected** MRS of $n$ robots | dimensionless |
| 2 | $d_{ij} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2}$ | Euclidean distance between robots $i$ and $j$ | meters (m) |
| 3 | $C_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1, & d_{ij} \le R_c \\ 0, & d_{ij} > R_c \end{cases}$ | Connectivity binary indicator | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}_k$ | Discrete-time linear state update for one robot | state vector $\in \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{z}_k = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{v}_k$, $\mathbf{v}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \mathbf{R})$ | Sensor measurement model with Gaussian noise | measurement $\in \mathbb{R}^m$ |
| 6 | $P_{connectivity} = 1 - e^{-\lambda \pi R_c^2}$ | Probability of network connectivity (Poisson disk model) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\omega_{motor} = \dfrac{V - I R_a}{K_e}$ | No-load angular velocity of a DC motor | rad/s |
| 8 | $\tau_{motor} = K_t \cdot I$ | Torque produced by a DC motor | N·m |
| 9 | $E_{batt} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Available battery energy | Watt-hours (Wh) |
| 10 | $T_{mission} \le \dfrac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}}$ | Maximum mission endurance | hours (h) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` in exam scripts to avoid LaTeX/markdown parsing issues in KTU digital answer sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

The "Components" framework underpins every co-operative robotics application:
- **Warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva / Locus Robotics) — homogeneous fleets of mobile bases with shared SLAM maps.
- **Search and Rescue (SAR)** — heterogeneous teams of UAVs (aerial scouts) + UGVs (ground carriers) + manipulators (rubble clearing).
- **Precision agriculture** — co-operative swarms of drones for multispectral crop monitoring.
- **Space exploration** — NASA CADRE project (Cooperative Autonomous Distributed Robotic Exploration) on the Moon.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Communication Link Count in a Multi-Robot System

**Problem:** A co-operative team of $n$ robots is **fully connected** (every robot can communicate directly with every other robot). How many unique communication links exist?

**Step 1 — Model the network as a graph.**
Represent each robot as a node in an **undirected graph** $G = (V, E)$. Each direct communication channel is an edge.

**Step 2 — Choose 2 endpoints per edge.**
Since the graph is undirected and we cannot have self-loops (a robot does not communicate with itself over the network), the number of edges equals the number of 2-element subsets of $n$ vertices.

$$
|E| = \binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n!}{2!(n-2)!}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the factorial expression.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|E| &= \dfrac{n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2)!}{2 \cdot (n-2)!} \\
&= \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the closed-form expression.**

$$
\boxed{N_{links} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This expression appears in KTU problems asking about "communication overhead," "bandwidth allocation," or "MQTT topic explosion." It scales **quadratically** with team size — a critical bottleneck for large swarms.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensor Measurement Update (Linear Gaussian Model)

**Problem:** A co-operative robot uses an onboard sensor to measure the range to a teammate. Derive the measurement update equation.

**Step 1 — Define the state vector.**
Let $\mathbf{x}_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \end{bmatrix}$ be the true 2D position of the teammate at time step $k$.

**Step 2 — Define the true (noiseless) measurement function.**

$$
h(\mathbf{x}_k) = \sqrt{(x_k - x_{ego})^2 + (y_k - y_{ego})^2}
$$

where $(x_{ego}, y_{ego})$ is the ego-robot's own position.

**Step 3 — Add zero-mean Gaussian noise.**

$$
z_k = h(\mathbf{x}_k) + v_k, \qquad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)
$$

Here $R = \sigma_r^2$ is the range measurement variance.

**Step 4 — Linearize using a first-order Taylor expansion around an estimate $\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k$.**

$$
h(\mathbf{x}_k) \approx h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k) + \mathbf{H}_k (\mathbf{x}_k - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_k)
$$

where the Jacobian (measurement matrix) is:

$$
\mathbf{H}_k = \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \bigg\vert_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}_k} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{x_k - x_{ego}}{\sqrt{(x_k - x_{ego})^2 + (y_k - y_{ego})^2}} & \dfrac{y_k - y_{ego}}{\sqrt{(x_k - x_{ego})^2 + (y_k - y_{ego})^2}} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Express the linearized measurement model.**

$$
z_k = \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{x}_k + v_k
$$

**Step 6 — State the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) update step** (the standard estimator for such co-operative localization):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k} &= \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1} + \mathbf{K}_k \bigl( z_k - h(\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{k \vert k-1}) \bigr) \\
\mathbf{K}_k &= \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^\top \bigl( \mathbf{H}_k \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1} \mathbf{H}_k^\top + R \bigr)^{-1} \\
\mathbf{P}_{k \vert k} &= (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{K}_k \mathbf{H}_k) \mathbf{P}_{k \vert k-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the mathematical backbone of **Co-operative Localization** (CL). The gain $\mathbf{K}_k$ quantifies how much to "trust" the incoming teammate measurement versus the robot's own dead-reckoning.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mission Endurance vs Battery Capacity

**Problem:** A delivery MRS of $n$ robots is dispatched. Each robot has a battery of capacity $C_{Ah}$ at nominal voltage $V_{nom}$ and consumes an average power $P_{avg}$. Derive the maximum mission endurance.

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the battery.**

$$
E_{batt} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} \quad [\text{Wh}]
$$

**Step 2 — Power balance over mission duration $T$.**

$$
E_{consumed} = P_{avg} \cdot T \quad [\text{Wh}]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the energy conservation constraint** $E_{batt} \ge E_{consumed}$.

$$
V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} \ge P_{avg} \cdot T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the maximum endurance.**

$$
T_{mission} \le \dfrac{V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}}{P_{avg}}
$$

**Step 5 — Example Numerical Evaluation:**
Given $V_{nom} = 11.1$ V, $C_{Ah} = 5$ Ah, $P_{avg} = 30$ W:

$$
T_{mission} = \dfrac{11.1 \times 5}{30} = \dfrac{55.5}{30} = 1.85 \text{ h}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{mission} \approx 1.85 \text{ h} = 111 \text{ minutes}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Trade-off

| Component Class | Cost | Power Draw | Computational Load | Co-operation Role |
|----------------|------|------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Differential drive base** | Low | Medium | Low | Transport |
| **Omnidirectional base** | High | High | Medium | Maneuvering in tight swarms |
| **2D LiDAR** | High | Medium | High (SLAM) | Shared mapping |
| **RGB-D Camera** | Medium | Medium | Very high (CNN) | Object detection |
| **IMU (6/9-DOF)** | Low | Very low | Low | Self-state estimation |
| **Wi-Fi module** | Low | Medium | Low | Inter-agent messaging |
| **NVIDIA Jetson Orin** | High | High (~25 W) | Very high | Onboard AI inference |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a co-operative robot for a SAR mission," use this matrix to justify trade-offs (e.g., prefer omnidirectional base + 2D LiDAR + 5G for open-field SAR; prefer tracked base + thermal camera + mesh radio for collapsed-building SAR).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Co-operative Robotic Agent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Subsystem Battery BMS] --> B[Onboard Compute SBC MCU FPGA]
    A --> C[Actuator Subsystem BLDC Servo Drivers]
    A --> D[Sensor Subsystem IMU LiDAR Camera GPS]
    B --> E[Communication Subsystem WiFi 5G BLE]
    D --> B
    B --> C
    E --> F[Network Coordinator ROS 2 DDS Middleware]
    F --> G[Teammate Agents MRS]
    B --> H[Mission Planner Task Allocator]
    H --> B
```

## 4.2 Multi-Robot Co-operation Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Layer 5 Mission and Task Allocation]
    L4[Layer 4 Coordination and Planning]
    L3[Layer 3 Communication and Messaging]
    L2[Layer 2 Perception and State Estimation]
    L1[Layer 1 Hardware Platform Sensors Actuators]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

## 4.3 Sensor Data Flow in a Co-operative SLAM Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Robot A LiDAR Scan] --> F1[Local SLAM Frontend]
    S2[Robot B LiDAR Scan] --> F2[Local SLAM Frontend]
    S3[Robot C Camera Image] --> F3[Visual Odometry]
    F1 --> M[ROS 2 Topic Bridge]
    F2 --> M
    F3 --> M
    M --> B[Backend Pose Graph Optimization]
    B --> MA[Global Map Shared via DDS]
    MA --> P1[Robot A Path Planner]
    MA --> P2[Robot B Path Planner]
    MA --> P3[Robot C Path Planner]
```

## 4.4 Component Interoperability Matrix (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AGENT[Co-operative Robot Agent]
        HW[Mechanical Base]
        SENS[Sensors]
        ACT[Actuators]
        COMP[Onboard Computer]
        COMM[Communication Radio]
    end
    subgraph TEAM[Teammate Network]
        T1[Teammate 1]
        T2[Teammate 2]
        TN[Teammate N]
    end
    HW --- ACT
    SENS --- COMP
    ACT --- COMP
    COMP --- COMM
    COMM <-->|Wireless Link| T1
    COMM <-->|Wireless Link| T2
    COMM <-->|Wireless Link| TN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Arrows denote **data flow** (not necessarily physical wiring). For example, in the sensor flow diagram, raw LiDAR scans feed the **Frontend**, processed features move to the **Backend**, and the optimized map is published back to all teammates for path planning.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Multi-Robot System (MRS). List any two advantages of co-operative robotics over single-robot systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Multi-Robot System (MRS)** is a team of two or more robotic agents that share a common environment, exchange information via a communication network, and coordinate their actions to accomplish individual or shared objectives.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Fault tolerance and redundancy** — If one robot fails, teammates can reallocate the task and continue mission execution, increasing overall robustness.
2. **Parallelism and scalability** — Multiple agents can explore, sense, or manipulate spatially distributed targets simultaneously, drastically reducing mission time.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Proprioceptive | Exteroceptive |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Measures** | Internal state of the robot | External environment |
| **Example** | Wheel encoder, IMU, gyroscope | LiDAR, RGB camera, GPS |
| **Shared in MRS?** | Rarely (each robot knows its own state) | Frequently (primary communication payload) |

*(1 mark for difference, 1 mark per example, 1 mark for MRS context.)*

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **five major components of a co-operative robotic system** and explain the role of each in achieving inter-agent collaboration. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A warehouse MRS consists of **6 identical mobile robots**. Compute the number of unique communication links if the network is (i) fully connected and (ii) arranged in a ring topology. Comment on the scalability implications. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Five major components:**

1. **Mechanical Platform / Body** – Provides physical structure; choice of locomotion (differential, Ackermann, quadrotor) determines maneuverability in the shared environment.
2. **Sensing Subsystem** – Acquires proprioceptive + exteroceptive data; LiDAR and cameras enable shared map construction.
3. **Actuation Subsystem** – Converts control commands into physical motion (BLDC motors, servo drivers).
4. **On-board Computation** – Runs SLAM, planning, and inter-agent coordination algorithms (e.g., ROS 2 nodes).
5. **Communication Subsystem** – Enables data exchange (Wi-Fi, 5G, Zigbee) — the **defining component** that distinguishes a "team" from a "collection."

*Block diagram: Refer to Section 4.1 above. (7 marks — 1 per component + 2 for the diagram and inter-connection explanation.)*

---

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**(i) Fully connected topology:**

$$
N_{links}^{full} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = \dfrac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ links}
$$

**(ii) Ring topology:**
Each robot connects only to its two nearest neighbors (one on each side). Thus:

$$
N_{links}^{ring} = n = 6 \text{ links}
$$

**Scalability comment:**
Fully connected topologies scale **quadratically** — for $n = 100$ robots, $N_{links} = 4950$, which quickly overwhelms bandwidth and CPU scheduling. Ring topologies scale **linearly** but suffer from single-point communication failures and longer routing paths. Real-world MRS therefore use **hybrid mesh** topologies (e.g., ROS 2 DDS discovery with ad-hoc Wi-Fi mesh).

*(3 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 1 mark for the scalability comment.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the **discrete-time state update equation** for a single robot in a co-operative team. How does this model extend to a multi-robot system using a stacked state vector? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile robot is powered by a **Li-Po battery (11.1 V, 5 Ah)** and consumes an **average power of 28 W** during a co-operative search mission. Compute the maximum mission endurance in minutes. If the team has **4 such robots** working in parallel, what is the total energy budget? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation:**

**Step 1** — Write the continuous-time dynamics of a single robot:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

**Step 2** — Discretize with sampling period $T_s$ using a zero-order hold:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{F}\mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{B}_d \mathbf{u}_k
$$

where $\mathbf{F} = e^{\mathbf{A} T_s}$ and $\mathbf{B}_d = \int_0^{T_s} e^{\mathbf{A}\tau} d\tau \cdot \mathbf{B}$.

**Step 3** — For a homogeneous team of $n$ robots, **stack** the individual states into one large vector:

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{x}_1 \\ \mathbf{x}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{x}_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \cdot n_x}
$$

**Step 4** — The team-level dynamics become:

$$
\mathbf{X}_{k+1} = (\mathbf{I}_n \otimes \mathbf{F}) \mathbf{X}_k + (\mathbf{I}_n \otimes \mathbf{B}_d) \mathbf{U}_k
$$

where $\otimes$ denotes the **Kronecker product** and $\mathbf{U}_k$ is the stacked control input.

**Step 5** — Inter-agent coupling (e.g., formation constraint $\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j = \mathbf{d}_{ij}^{desired}$) is added as a **consensus term**:

$$
\mathbf{U}_k = \mathbf{K}_{local}\mathbf{X}_k + \mathbf{K}_{consensus} \mathbf{L} \mathbf{X}_k
$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the **graph Laplacian** of the communication topology.

*(7 marks — 2 for single robot, 3 for stacking, 2 for consensus extension.)*

---

**(b) Numerical solution:**

**Step 1** — Battery energy per robot:

$$
E_{batt} = 11.1 \text{ V} \times 5 \text{ Ah} = 55.5 \text{ Wh}
$$

**Step 2** — Endurance per robot:

$$
T_{mission} = \dfrac{55.5}{28} = 1.982 \text{ h} \approx 118.93 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3** — Team endurance per robot: **≈ 119 minutes** (each robot runs independently).

**Step 4** — Total team energy budget:

$$
E_{team} = 4 \times 55.5 = 222 \text{ Wh}
$$

*(3 marks for endurance, 2 marks for total energy, 2 marks for unit consistency and interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit consistency:** Many students lose **1–2 marks** by mixing **Wh** and **Ah**, or reporting endurance in hours when the question asks for minutes. Always **state the unit in every step**.
> - **Topological assumptions:** In link-count problems, students frequently forget that a ring with $n$ robots has exactly $n$ edges (not $n-1$ for open chain, **$n$ for the closed ring**). Read the topology carefully.
> - **Diagram labelling:** In the 7-mark block diagram question, students often draw boxes without arrows. KTU examiners expect **labelled arrows** showing data and power flow. Missing labels cost up to **2 marks**.
> - **Stacking vs coupling:** In the multi-robot dynamics question, the step from single-robot $\mathbf{x}_k$ to team-level $\mathbf{X}_k$ via Kronecker product is the **most skipped** step and the most penalized — always show the explicit block structure.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Co-operative Robotics = MRS + Inter-agent Communication + Shared Goal.** A team is not a team without a communication backbone.
- **Five component pillars:** Platform, Sensors, Actuators, Compute, Communication. Memorize the 5-layer architecture (Section 4.2).
- **Fully connected MRS has $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ links** — a quadratic scalability bottleneck.
- **Linear Gaussian sensor model:** $z_k = H x_k + v_k, \, v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$. This is the foundation of co-operative EKF localization.
- **EKF measurement update:** Master the three coupled equations for state, Kalman gain, and covariance.
- **Battery endurance formula:** $T = \dfrac{V \cdot C_{Ah}}{P_{avg}}$ — always check unit conversions (V × Ah = Wh, then divide by W to get hours).
- **Graph Laplacian** $\mathbf{L}$ encodes the communication topology and appears in consensus and formation control derivations.
- **Kronecker product** $(\mathbf{I}_n \otimes \mathbf{F})$ is the standard way to lift a single-robot model to a multi-robot team.
- **Proprioceptive vs Exteroceptive** is a high-frequency 3-mark question — answer with a table.
- **Always include a labelled block diagram** in 7-mark "describe" questions — it is worth 2 marks on its own.
- **Watch units:** Wh vs Ah vs W vs minutes/hours — KTU examiners deduct for silent unit errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Odometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Odometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1 — Odometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 1: Robot Localization & Odometry* of PERUT861 (Co-operative Robotics). It is a **high-yield unit** for KTU ESE and internal assessments, frequently appearing as a **14-mark Part B question**.

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)

**Odometry** is a *dead-reckoning* method for estimating a mobile robot's instantaneous pose (position $(x, y)$ and orientation $\theta$) by **integrating incremental wheel rotations** measured by wheel encoders over time. Since the encoders are mounted directly on the robot's drive wheels, odometry is a **proprioceptive**, **local**, and **incremental** localization technique that operates entirely from the robot's internal sensors without requiring any external reference (no beacons, GPS, or landmarks).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, odometry is studied under the **forward kinematics of wheeled mobile robots (WMRs)**, where it serves as the foundational baseline against which all cooperative multi-robot localization methods (e.g., EKF-based cooperative localization, graph SLAM) are benchmarked.

The robot's pose at time $t$ is conventionally represented as:

$$\mathbf{p}_t = \begin{bmatrix} x_t \\ y_t \\ \theta_t \end{bmatrix}$$

where $(x_t, y_t)$ is the planar position in the world (global) frame and $\theta_t$ is the heading angle measured counter-clockwise from the global $x$-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Odo-meter"?** The word comes from the Greek *hodos* (road/path) and *metron* (measure) — literally "road measurement." In mobile robotics it generalizes to "self-measurement of the path traveled."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are blindfolded inside a familiar room and asked to walk from the door to the table. You cannot see the room. All you have are:

1. Two pedometers strapped to your ankles (one on each foot) — these are the **wheel encoders**.
2. Your memory of how far apart your feet usually step — this is the **wheelbase** $b$.

You start counting steps. Every time your right foot moves a fixed distance $d_r$, you tick a counter $n_r$. Same for the left foot: $n_l \cdot d_l$. By knowing:
- How far each foot has traveled in total, and
- The distance between your feet,

you can mathematically reconstruct *approximately* where you are and which way you are facing.

This "step-counting" approach to localization is **exactly what odometry does for a robot**. The two wheels are the two feet, the encoders count the "steps," and the math reconstructs the pose.

> [!TIP]
> **Critical insight for students:** Like the blindfolded person, odometry is **fundamentally cumulative** — small errors at each step *accumulate* over time and over distance. This is the central limitation that motivates the **cooperative robotics** angle of this course (robots sharing sensor information to bound odometric drift).

## 1.3 Standard Wheel-Encoder Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Encoder counts per wheel revolution | $N_c$ | counts/rev |
| Wheel radius | $r$ | **m** |
| Wheel circumference | $C = 2\pi r$ | **m/rev** |
| Resolution (meters per tick) | $\rho = C / N_c$ | m/count |
| Wheelbase (axle length) | $b$ | **m** |
| Encoder count (left / right) | $n_l, n_r$ | counts |
| Update interval | $\Delta t$ | s |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Odometric Kinematics

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Bulleted Logic)

Odometry in a **differential-drive robot (DDR)** proceeds through a clean four-stage pipeline:

1. **Sampling the encoders** at time $t$: read the integer tick counts $n_r(t)$ and $n_l(t)$.
2. **Differencing** to get incremental counts:
   $$\Delta n_r = n_r(t) - n_r(t - \Delta t), \quad \Delta n_l = n_l(t) - n_l(t - \Delta t)$$
3. **Converting ticks to arc-lengths traveled** by each wheel:
   $$\Delta s_r = \rho \cdot \Delta n_r, \quad \Delta s_l = \rho \cdot \Delta n_l$$
   where $\rho = 2\pi r / N_c$ is the encoder's linear resolution.
4. **Forward integration** through the kinematic model to update the pose.

## 2.2 The Core Differential-Drive Kinematic Equations

For a two-wheeled differential-drive robot, the **instantaneous center of rotation (ICR)** lies on the wheelbase axis. Let:

- $\Delta s_r$ = distance rolled by the right wheel in interval $\Delta t$
- $\Delta s_l$ = distance rolled by the left wheel in interval $\Delta t$
- $b$ = wheelbase (distance between contact patches of left and right wheels)
- $\Delta \theta$ = change in heading

Then by simple arc-length geometry on the ICR:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$$

The forward displacement of the robot's midpoint along its **current heading** is the average of the two wheel displacements:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the average?** The midpoint of the axle travels the *arithmetic mean* of the two arc-lengths, because each wheel traces an arc of the same angle $\Delta \theta$ but of different radii $\left(b/2 + b/2\right)$ away from the ICR.

## 2.3 Pose Update — Three Equivalent Formulations

There are three textbook ways to write the pose update. They differ in numerical accuracy and complexity:

### (a) Exact (mid-point) formulation — *recommended for KTU answers*

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) First-order Euler (small-angle) approximation — *often used in EKF prediction*

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &\approx x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos(\theta_t) \\
y_{t+1} &\approx y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin(\theta_t) \\
\theta_{t+1} &\approx \theta_t + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) Velocity-based (continuous-time) form

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos \theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin \theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

with $v = (v_r + v_l)/2$ and $\omega = (v_r - v_l)/b$, where $v_r, v_l$ are the right and left wheel linear velocities.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical Part B questions on odometry.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel displacement (right) | $\Delta s_r = \rho \cdot \Delta n_r = \dfrac{2\pi r}{N_c}\,\Delta n_r$ | m |
| 2 | Wheel displacement (left) | $\Delta s_l = \rho \cdot \Delta n_l$ | m |
| 3 | Heading change | $\Delta \theta = \dfrac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}$ | rad |
| 4 | Forward displacement | $\Delta s = \dfrac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$ | m |
| 5 | Linear velocity | $v = \dfrac{v_r + v_l}{2}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{v_r - v_l}{b}$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Pose $x$ update | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$ | m |
| 8 | Pose $y$ update | $y_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$ | m |
| 9 | Pose $\theta$ update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t + \Delta \theta$ | rad |
| 10 | Encoder resolution | $\rho = \dfrac{2\pi r}{N_c}$ | m/count |
| 11 | Radius of curvature | $R = \dfrac{b\,(\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)}{2(\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)}$ | m |
| 12 | Distance traveled | $D = \sum_{k} \sqrt{\Delta x_k^2 + \Delta y_k^2}$ | m |

> **Symbol safeguard:** where an absolute value or modulus is needed (e.g., in error formulas), write it as $\vert x \vert$ so as **not** to break the markdown table above.

## 2.5 Sources of Odometric Error (Theory-Only)

Because errors compound, KTU expects you to be able to **list and explain** the dominant non-systematic and systematic errors:

- **Systematic errors** (deterministic, repeatable):
  * Unequal wheel diameters ($\Delta r$)
  * Misalignment of wheels (effective wheelbase ≠ nominal $b$)
  * Finite encoder resolution (quantization $\rho$)
  * Sampling rate limits
- **Non-systematic errors** (stochastic):
  * Wheel slip on the floor
  * Traversing uneven terrain
  * Bumps, debris hitting wheels

> [!TIP]
> **Cooperative-robotics link:** The *whole point* of cooperative localization (later modules) is that when Robot A meets Robot B and they *observe* each other, they can use the relative measurement to **re-anchor** their odometric estimates and reset the accumulated drift. Keep this in mind when studying later modules.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse AMRs** (Amazon Kiva, Fetch Robotics) use odometry as the backbone of their localization stack, fused with LiDAR/IMU via a particle filter.
- **Autonomous cars** use *visual odometry* and *wheel odometry* as redundant channels feeding a Kalman filter.
- **Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance)**: their entire long-baseline traverse over many sols is reconstructed from a combination of visual odometry and proprioceptive wheel-encoder odometry.
- **Differential-drive educational kits** (TurtleBot, e-puck, LEGO Mindstorms) — exactly the platform assumed by KTU's Module 1.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analyses

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Wheel Kinematics to Pose Update

**Starting point:** A differential-drive robot with two coaxial driven wheels separated by distance $b$. Wheel rotations are sampled at intervals $\Delta t$. Encoder counts increment by $\Delta n_r$ (right) and $\Delta n_l$ (left).

**Step 1 — Convert counts to arc length.**

Each wheel has radius $r$ and the encoder fires $N_c$ counts per full revolution. The arc length corresponding to one count is the resolution:

$$\rho = \frac{\text{wheel circumference}}{\text{counts per revolution}} = \frac{2\pi r}{N_c}$$

Hence the distances rolled in interval $\Delta t$ are:

$$\Delta s_r = \rho \cdot \Delta n_r = \frac{2\pi r}{N_c}\,\Delta n_r$$

$$\Delta s_l = \rho \cdot \Delta n_l = \frac{2\pi r}{N_c}\,\Delta n_l$$

**Step 2 — Relate arc lengths to heading change.**

The right wheel traces an arc of radius $R_r = R + b/2$ and the left wheel an arc of radius $R_l = R - b/2$, where $R$ is the ICR radius measured from the robot's midline. Both arcs subtend the **same** angle $\Delta \theta$ (rigid body assumption):

$$\Delta s_r = R_r \cdot \Delta \theta = \left(R + \frac{b}{2}\right) \Delta \theta$$

$$\Delta s_l = R_l \cdot \Delta \theta = \left(R - \frac{b}{2}\right) \Delta \theta$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the unknown $R$ by subtraction.**

$$\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l = b \cdot \Delta \theta$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate the unknown $R$ by addition.**

$$\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l = 2 R \cdot \Delta \theta$$

The midpoint travels the distance $\Delta s = R \cdot \Delta \theta$, so:

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Update the global pose.**

The midpoint displacement in the world frame is obtained by rotating the body-frame vector $(\Delta s, 0)$ by the heading angle that prevailed *during* the interval. Using the trapezoidal (mid-point) rule, that effective heading is $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$:

$$\Delta x_w = \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

$$\Delta y_w = \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right)$$

Therefore the pose update is:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cdot \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \cdot \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta \theta}{2}\right) \\
\theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta \theta
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **exact discrete-time odometry equation** for a differential-drive robot. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Continuous-Time Velocity Form

Define wheel linear velocities as time derivatives of arc length:

$$v_r = \frac{d s_r}{dt}, \qquad v_l = \frac{d s_l}{dt}$$

Differentiating the discrete-time equations with respect to time:

$$\omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \frac{v_r - v_l}{b}$$

$$v = \frac{d s}{dt} = \frac{v_r + v_l}{2}$$

The robot's midline moves in the direction of its current heading $\theta$ with speed $v$ and rotates at rate $\omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v \cos \theta \\
\dot{y} &= v \sin \theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating over $[t, t+\Delta t]$ recovers the discrete form of §3.1. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Variant)

**Problem.** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.05$ m, encoder resolution $N_c = 500$ counts/rev, and wheelbase $b = 0.30$ m. It starts at pose $(x_0, y_0, \theta_0) = (0, 0, 0)$. In one control interval, the encoder counts change by $\Delta n_r = 60$ and $\Delta n_l = 40$. Compute the new pose using the exact mid-point formulation.

**Step 1 — Compute resolution.**

$$\rho = \frac{2\pi r}{N_c} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.05}{500} = \frac{0.1\pi}{500} = 6.2832 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/count}$$

**Step 2 — Wheel displacements.**

$$\Delta s_r = \rho \cdot \Delta n_r = (6.2832 \times 10^{-4})(60) = 0.03770 \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta s_l = \rho \cdot \Delta n_l = (6.2832 \times 10^{-4})(40) = 0.02513 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Heading change.**

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b} = \frac{0.03770 - 0.02513}{0.30} = \frac{0.01257}{0.30} = 0.04190 \text{ rad} \approx 2.401^\circ$$

**Step 4 — Forward displacement.**

$$\Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2} = \frac{0.03770 + 0.02513}{2} = 0.03142 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Mid-point heading.**

$$\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 0 + \frac{0.04190}{2} = 0.02095 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 6 — Apply exact pose update.**

$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.03142 \times \cos(0.02095) = 0.03142 \times 0.99978 = 0.03141 \text{ m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.03142 \times \sin(0.02095) = 0.03142 \times 0.02094 = 0.000658 \text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.04190 = 0.04190 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final pose:**

$$\boxed{\;(x_1,\, y_1,\, \theta_1) \;=\; (0.03141 \text{ m},\; 0.000658 \text{ m},\; 0.04190 \text{ rad})\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Compare with Euler:** if we had used the naive Euler form (cos and sin of $\theta_t = 0$), we would get $x_1 = 0.03142$, $y_1 = 0$, missing the small lateral drift. The exact form correctly captures it.

## 3.4 Derivation III — Radius of Curvature (KTU Favourite)

The instantaneous center-of-rotation radius from the robot midline is obtained from the equations of §3.1:

$$R = \frac{b(\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)}{2(\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)} = \frac{b \cdot \Delta s}{\Delta \theta \cdot \tfrac{b}{2}}$$

A useful sanity check:

- If $\Delta s_r = \Delta s_l$: $\Delta \theta = 0$, $R \to \infty$ — robot moves in a **straight line**.
- If $\Delta s_l = 0$: the robot **pivots about the left wheel**; $R = b/2$.
- If $\Delta s_l = -\Delta s_l$: pure **in-place rotation**; $R = 0$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Differential-Drive Geometry (Reference Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Frame["World Frame X-Y"]
        Xaxis["X axis"]
        Yaxis["Y axis"]
    end

    subgraph Robot["Robot Body"]
        WL["Left Wheel\nradius r\narc Ds_l"]
        WR["Right Wheel\nradius r\narc Ds_r"]
        Mid["Midpoint M\npose x y theta"]
        WL -- "b/2" --- Mid
        Mid -- "b/2" --- WR
    end

    ICR["Instantaneous Center\nof Rotation ICR\nradius R"]
    WL -. "arc R - b/2" .- ICR
    WR -. "arc R + b/2" .- ICR
    Mid -. "arc R" .- ICR

    Mid -- "heading theta" --> Frame
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- The two wheels sit on a common axle of length $b$.
- The ICR lies on the extension of the axle. For straight-line motion it is at infinity; for in-place turns it lies exactly at the midpoint $M$.
- The mid-point $M$ is the point whose pose $(x,y,\theta)$ the odometry equations integrate.

## 4.2 Odometry Computation Pipeline (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1\nRead encoders at time t\nn_r t and n_l t"]
    B["Step 2\nCompute tick deltas\nDn_r = n_r t minus n_r t-1\nDn_l = n_l t minus n_l t-1"]
    C["Step 3\nConvert ticks to distances\nDs_r = rho times Dn_r\nDs_l = rho times Dn_l"]
    D["Step 4\nCompute heading change\nDtheta = Ds_r minus Ds_l over b"]
    E["Step 5\nCompute forward travel\nDs = Ds_r plus Ds_l over 2"]
    F["Step 6\nExact pose update\nx new = x old plus Ds cos theta plus Dtheta/2\ny new = y old plus Ds sin theta plus Dtheta/2\ntheta new = theta old plus Dtheta"]
    G["Output\nNew pose p sub t plus 1"]

    A --> B --> C --> D
    C --> E
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define odometry. Mention two systematic and two non-systematic sources of odometric error.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (target ~150 words):**

Odometry is a proprioceptive dead-reckoning method of estimating a wheeled mobile robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ by integrating wheel rotations read from on-board shaft encoders over successive time intervals.

Systematic (deterministic) sources of error:

1. Unequal effective wheel diameters due to manufacturing tolerances.
2. Finite encoder resolution (one tick corresponds to a non-zero $\rho$, producing quantization noise).

Non-systematic (stochastic) sources of error:

1. Wheel slip on slippery or uneven terrain.
2. Traversing bumps or floor debris that perturb the contact patch.

These errors are cumulative; without external correction, pose uncertainty grows without bound.

### Q2. Why is the mid-point (exact) odometry update preferred over the simple Euler update?  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (target ~120 words):**

The mid-point update evaluates $\cos$ and $\sin$ at the **effective heading** $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ — the average orientation during the interval — rather than at the start-of-interval heading $\theta_t$. For non-infinitesimal $\Delta \theta$ this preserves the chord length of the robot's curved trajectory exactly, whereas the Euler form systematically truncates the path. The Euler update therefore introduces a per-step bias in $(x,y)$ that is $O(\Delta\theta^2)$ and grows unboundedly over time. For any non-trivial rotation, the exact form is both more accurate and only negligibly more expensive to compute.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice Question (14 Marks)

> Per **KTU ESE pattern**: students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ⬇️ Question A (14 Marks) — Derivation-Focused

#### (a) Derive the exact pose-update equations for a differential-drive mobile robot from encoder readings. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 marks]**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Robot moves on a flat, horizontal plane.
- Wheels roll without slipping during the interval.
- Wheelbase $b$ and wheel radius $r$ are known constants.
- The rigid body rotates about a single instantaneous center of rotation (ICR).

**Derivation outline** (6 marks — credit per logical step):

1. Convert encoder count deltas to arc lengths using resolution $\rho = 2\pi r / N_c$:
   $$\Delta s_r = \rho\,\Delta n_r, \qquad \Delta s_l = \rho\,\Delta n_l$$

2. Express each arc length as the product of its ICR radius and the common swept angle $\Delta\theta$:
   $$\Delta s_r = (R + b/2)\Delta\theta, \quad \Delta s_l = (R - b/2)\Delta\theta$$

3. Subtract and add to eliminate $R$:
   $$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}, \qquad \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$$

4. Rotate the body-frame displacement $(\Delta s, 0)$ into the world frame using the mid-interval heading, then add to current pose:
   $$\begin{aligned}
   x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s\,\cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
   y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s\,\sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
   \theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
   \end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **explicitly state assumptions** — losing up to **1 mark** automatically. Also, do *not* skip the geometric reasoning of Step 2; merely writing down the final formulas without the ICR argument earns **partial credit only** (typically capped at 4/7).

#### (b) A differential-drive robot with $r = 0.04$ m, $N_c = 2000$ counts/rev, and $b = 0.25$ m is initially at $(0, 0, 0)$. In one interval the encoder deltas are $\Delta n_r = 80$ and $\Delta n_l = 20$. Compute the new pose using the exact formulation. **[7 marks]**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Resolution.**
$$\rho = \frac{2\pi \times 0.04}{2000} = \frac{0.08\pi}{2000} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/count}$$

**Step 2 — Wheel displacements.**
$$\Delta s_r = \rho \cdot 80 = 0.01005 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta s_l = \rho \cdot 20 = 0.002513 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Heading change.**
$$\Delta\theta = \frac{0.01005 - 0.002513}{0.25} = \frac{0.007540}{0.25} = 0.03016 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Forward travel.**
$$\Delta s = \frac{0.01005 + 0.002513}{2} = 0.006283 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Mid-point heading.**
$$\theta_t + \frac{\Delta\theta}{2} = 0.01508 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 6 — Pose update.**
$$\begin{aligned}
x_1 &= 0 + 0.006283 \cos(0.01508) = 0.006283 \times 0.99989 = 0.006282 \text{ m} \\
y_1 &= 0 + 0.006283 \sin(0.01508) = 0.006283 \times 0.01508 = 9.475 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \\
\theta_1 &= 0 + 0.03016 = 0.03016 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final pose:** $(x_1, y_1, \theta_1) = (6.282 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m},\ 9.475 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m},\ 0.03016 \text{ rad})$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1:** Students often drop the mid-point angle and use $\theta_t = 0$, giving $y_1 = 0$. This is the Euler approximation, which loses **1 mark** in a "exact formulation" question.
> **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting units on $\rho$ and leaving $N_c$ in the denominator is the most common slip-up — always carry units symbolically.

---

### ⬇️ Question B (14 Marks) — Theory + Numerics

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the wheel-encoder-based odometry pipeline for a differential-drive robot. **[7 marks]**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution (textual — diagram covered in §4.2 above):**

The odometry pipeline consists of four sequential blocks (each ≈1.5–2 marks):

1. **Encoder acquisition block.** Shaft encoders mounted on the motors return integer counts $n_r(t)$, $n_l(t)$ at fixed sampling rate $1/\Delta t$.

2. **Differencing & conversion block.** Tick deltas $\Delta n_r, \Delta n_l$ are computed and converted into per-wheel arc lengths via the encoder resolution $\rho = 2\pi r / N_c$:
   $$\Delta s_r = \rho\,\Delta n_r, \qquad \Delta s_l = \rho\,\Delta n_l$$

3. **Differential kinematics block.** Using the wheelbase $b$, the heading change and forward travel are computed:
   $$\Delta\theta = \frac{\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l}{b}, \qquad \Delta s = \frac{\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l}{2}$$

4. **Pose integration block.** The current pose $(x_t, y_t, \theta_t)$ is updated using the exact mid-point rule from §3.1:
   $$\begin{aligned}
   x_{t+1} &= x_t + \Delta s \cos\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
   y_{t+1} &= y_t + \Delta s \sin\!\left(\theta_t + \tfrac{\Delta\theta}{2}\right) \\
   \theta_{t+1} &= \theta_t + \Delta\theta
   \end{aligned}$$

The output is the new pose estimate $\mathbf{p}_{t+1}$, which is fed back as input for the next iteration.

#### (b) Compare wheel odometry with visual odometry. Why is odometry insufficient as a stand-alone localization method for cooperative multi-robot systems? **[7 marks]**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wheel Odometry | Visual Odometry |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor | Wheel shaft encoder | Camera (mono / stereo / RGB-D) |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate to high |
| Frame rate | High (kHz) | Moderate (30–60 Hz) |
| Robustness to slip | Poor | Excellent |
| Robustness to lighting | N/A | Poor in dark / glare |
| Drift | High on long paths | Comparable, scene-dependent |
| Information richness | 1-D per wheel | High-dimensional feature tracks |

**Why odometry alone is insufficient for cooperative systems (4 marks):**

1. **Unbounded drift.** Because odometry is an *integration* of incremental measurements, all encoder noise (quantization, slip) accumulates without bound. Without an absolute reference, the variance of the pose estimate grows superlinearly with distance traveled.

2. **No common reference frame.** Each robot's odometric estimate is referenced to its own starting pose. For two robots to *cooperate* (e.g., rendezvous, formation control, cooperative SLAM), they need a **shared frame**, which requires inter-robot relative observations or absolute landmarks.

3. **No loop closure.** When a robot returns to a previously visited location, odometry alone cannot recognize the revisit. Cooperative systems use re-observation events between robots to **close loops** and reset drift.

4. **Coupling of state and observability.** In cooperative localization, one robot's pose becomes an observable for another via relative measurement; pure odometry provides no such coupling, so the multi-robot observability matrix remains rank-deficient.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Question B(b):** A common student error is to list only "drift" as the reason. To get full marks, explicitly connect the answer to the **cooperative** aspect of the course — i.e., explain *why* odometry fails when *multiple* robots must share information, not just for a single robot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this on your wall the night before the exam.**

- ☐ Odometry = dead-reckoning from **wheel encoder counts** (proprioceptive).
- ☐ Pose is $\mathbf{p} = (x, y, \theta)^T$; encoder resolution is $\rho = 2\pi r / N_c$.
- ☐ Two key derived quantities: $\Delta\theta = (\Delta s_r - \Delta s_l)/b$ and $\Delta s = (\Delta s_r + \Delta s_l)/2$.
- ☐ Exact mid-point pose update uses $\cos$ and $\sin$ of $\theta_t + \Delta\theta/2$ — **do not omit the half-angle** in ESE answers.
- ☐ Euler approximation is only valid for $\Delta\theta \ll 1$ rad.
- ☐ ICR geometry: straight line $\Leftrightarrow \Delta\theta = 0$; in-place turn $\Leftrightarrow R = 0$.
- ☐ Wheel odometry sources of error: systematic (wheel diameter mismatch, encoder quantization, misalignment) and non-systematic (slip, terrain).
- ☐ Odometry drift is **unbounded** without external corrections — this is the central motivation for **cooperative localization** in later modules.
- ☐ For KTU Part B: always **list assumptions**, **show each algebraic step**, and **carry units** explicitly.
- ☐ Memorize the formula sheet in §2.4 cold — it unlocks nearly every numerical in the module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kinematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Kinematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Co-operative Robotics**, *Kinematics* refers to the study of the **geometric and time-based motion** of multi-robot manipulator systems that physically interact with each other, either directly (e.g., robot-to-robot contact) or indirectly (e.g., co-manipulating a common rigid object), **without considering the forces that cause the motion**.

Formally, cooperative kinematics establishes the **mathematical mapping** between the **joint space** (configuration variables of all robots in the cooperative system) and the **cooperative task space** (the Cartesian space of the object being manipulated, or the relative configuration between the end-effectors).

For a system of **n** robots co-manipulating a single rigid object, the cooperative kinematic problem is expressed as:

$$
\mathbf{x}_o = \mathbf{f}_o(\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{q}_n)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}_o \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the object's pose vector and $\mathbf{q}_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n_i}$ is the joint vector of the $i$-th robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Definition of "Co-operative"**: Two or more robots are said to operate *co-operatively* when their motion is **explicitly coupled through a shared task constraint**, typically the rigid-body constraint imposed by a commonly held object or by physical contact.

## 1.2 Types of Cooperative Kinematic Problems

| Sub-Problem | Description |
|---|---|
| **Forward Cooperative Kinematics** | Given all joint angles $\mathbf{q}$, determine the object pose $\mathbf{x}_o$. |
| **Inverse Cooperative Kinematics** | Given the desired object trajectory $\mathbf{x}_o(t)$, determine the joint trajectories $\mathbf{q}_i(t)$ for all robots. |
| **Cooperative Velocity Kinematics** | Relate joint velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_i$ to the object velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o$ via the cooperative Jacobian. |
| **Relative Kinematics** | Describe the motion of one robot's end-effector relative to another's. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Two Movers Carrying a Sofa"**
> Imagine two people (Robot 1 and Robot 2) carrying a heavy sofa (the common object) up a staircase. They cannot act independently — if Person 1 walks faster or tilts, Person 2 **must** adjust to keep the sofa from bending, twisting, or falling. The cooperative kinematics of the system is essentially the *mathematical contract* that says: *"If you move your arms this way, I will move mine that way, such that the sofa remains rigid and its net velocity in the room equals some desired value."*
>
> In engineering terms: the **individual arm joint angles** (input) must combine to produce a **single coherent sofa motion** (output), governed by geometric rules derived from the sofa's geometry and the grip points.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standard** in cooperative kinematic analysis (KTU 2024 Scheme):

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a single manipulator: typically **6** (3 translation + 3 rotation) for full spatial operation.
- **Cooperative DOF** for two 6-DOF arms manipulating a rigid object: **6** (the object has 6 DOF in space).
- **Redundancy** in the cooperative system: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i - m$, where $m$ is the object DOF.
- **Grasp Matrix** $\mathbf{G}_i$: a $6 \times 6$ matrix relating the $i$-th end-effector's wrench to the object's wrench.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Cooperative Kinematic Problem

The cooperative kinematic problem for **two robotic arms** co-manipulating a single rigid object can be broken into the following logical layers:

### Step 1 — Individual Robot Forward Kinematics
For each robot, the standard DH-based forward kinematics gives the end-effector pose as a function of its own joints:
$$
\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{f}_1(\mathbf{q}_1), \qquad \mathbf{x}_2 = \mathbf{f}_2(\mathbf{q}_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Grasp / Contact Constraint
Because the object is rigid, the velocity of each end-effector **must equal** the velocity of the corresponding contact point on the object. This couples both robots to the single object:
$$
\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{v}_o + \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \times \mathbf{r}_{1/o}, \qquad
\mathbf{v}_2 = \mathbf{v}_o + \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \times \mathbf{r}_{2/o}
$$
where $\mathbf{r}_{i/o}$ is the position vector from the object frame to the $i$-th grasp point.

### Step 3 — Cooperative Velocity Mapping
Differentiating the constraint equations yields the **cooperative Jacobian relationship**:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o = \mathbf{J}_o(\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2) 
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{q}}_1 \\ \dot{\mathbf{q}}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $\mathbf{J}_o$ is the **cooperative (object) Jacobian** of size $6 \times (n_1 + n_2)$.

### Step 4 — Inverse Cooperative Kinematics
The required joint velocities to realize a desired object velocity are obtained via:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}_o^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}}_o + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}_o^{+}\mathbf{J}_o)\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$
where $\mathbf{J}_o^{+}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse, and the second term is a null-space vector representing **internal motions** (e.g., squeezing the object, repositioning fingers).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{f}_i(\mathbf{q}_i)$ | Individual forward kinematics | $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^6$ |
| 2 | $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i)\dot{\mathbf{q}}_i$ | Single-arm differential kinematics | $\mathbf{J}_i$ : $6 \times n_i$ |
| 3 | $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o$ | Grasp constraint (linear part) | $\mathbf{G}_i$ : grasp matrix |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o = \mathbf{J}_o \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Cooperative object velocity | $\mathbf{J}_o = [\mathbf{G}_1^{-1}\mathbf{J}_1 \;,\; \mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\mathbf{J}_2]$ |
| 5 | $\mathbf{J}_o = \mathbf{W}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{J}_1 \\ \mathbf{J}_2\end{bmatrix}$ | Weighted cooperative Jacobian | $\mathbf{W}$ : weighting matrix |
| 6 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}_o^{+}\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o + \mathbf{N}\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ | Inverse differential cooperative kinematics | $\mathbf{N} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}_o^{+}\mathbf{J}_o$ |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}_o^{T} \mathbf{F}_o$ | Static force relationship | $\mathbf{F}_o$ : object wrench |
| 8 | $\sum_{i=1}^{n}\mathbf{G}_i^{T}\mathbf{F}_i = \mathbf{F}_o$ | Object wrench from grip wrenches | $\mathbf{F}_i$ : end-effector wrench |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems**:
> - **Tight grasp (rigid grasp)**: $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o$ — no slip, no relative motion at contact.
> - **Loose grasp**: $\mathbf{v}_i \neq \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o$ — included for advanced problems only.
> - All revolute joints assumed unless stated otherwise.
> - Object frame is conventionally placed at the **center of mass** for force analysis and at a **geometric centroid** for kinematic simplicity.

## 2.3 Why and How — The Engineering Rationale

- **Why a cooperative Jacobian?** Because a single object has only 6 DOF but the system has $n_1 + n_2$ joint DOF. The cooperative Jacobian is the bridge that converts high-dimensional joint motion into the low-dimensional task-space motion that actually matters (the object moving through the room).
- **How redundancy helps**: With two 6-DOF arms, the system has 12 joints to control 6 object DOF — leaving **6 internal DOFs** that can be used to optimize secondary objectives: avoid joint limits, avoid singularities, minimize energy, or apply internal squeezing forces on the object for secure handling.
- **Real-world utility**: Industrial assembly of large workpieces (car bodies, aircraft wings), dual-arm surgical robots (da Vinci surgical system), and planetary rover teams that lift samples together.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Cooperative Object Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_o$

We start with the assumption that **two robotic arms** with $n_1$ and $n_2$ revolute joints are rigidly grasping a single object at grasp points $A$ (on robot 1) and $B$ (on robot 2). Let $\mathbf{q} = [\mathbf{q}_1^{T}, \mathbf{q}_2^{T}]^{T}$ be the stacked joint vector.

### Step A — Write the velocity constraint for each end-effector

The linear and angular velocity of the $i$-th end-effector (treated as a frame attached to the object) is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{e} = \mathbf{v}_o + \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \times \mathbf{r}_{i/o}
$$

In stacked 6-vector form (linear on top, angular on bottom), this becomes:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_i \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o}) \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_o \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{S}(\cdot)$ is the $3 \times 3$ skew-symmetric cross-product matrix, and the $6 \times 6$ block matrix on the right is the **grasp matrix** $\mathbf{G}_i$.

### Step B — Define the grasp matrix explicitly

$$
\mathbf{G}_i = 
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_{3} & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o}) \\ \mathbf{0}_{3} & \mathbf{I}_{3} \end{bmatrix}
$$

For two arms, we write:

$$
\mathbf{G}_1 = 
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{1/o}) \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad
\mathbf{G}_2 = 
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{2/o}) \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step C — Relate end-effector velocity to joint velocities

For each robot, the standard differential kinematics gives:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i^{e} = \mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_i
$$

Equating the two expressions for $\mathbf{v}_i^{e}$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_i = \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o
$$

where $\mathbf{v}_o = [\mathbf{v}_o^{T}, \boldsymbol{\omega}_o^{T}]^{T}$.

### Step D — Solve for the object velocity

If $\mathbf{G}_i$ is invertible (which it is, since $\mathbf{G}_i^{-1} = \mathbf{G}_i^{T}$ for this structure), we obtain:

$$
\mathbf{v}_o = \mathbf{G}_i^{-1} \mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_i
$$

For two arms, stacking gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{v}_o &= \mathbf{G}_1^{-1} \mathbf{J}_1(\mathbf{q}_1) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_1 \\
\mathbf{v}_o &= \mathbf{G}_2^{-1} \mathbf{J}_2(\mathbf{q}_2) \dot{\mathbf{q}}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step E — Combine into a single cooperative relationship

Writing both equations together as one over-determined system and solving in a least-squares sense (with optional weighting $\mathbf{W}$):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o = \mathbf{v}_o = \mathbf{J}_o(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

where the **cooperative Jacobian** is:

$$
\mathbf{J}_o = \underbrace{\left(\mathbf{G}_1^{-T}\mathbf{G}_1^{-1} + \mathbf{G}_2^{-T}\mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\right)^{-1}}_{\text{weighted pseudoinverse term}}
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{G}_1^{-1}\mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

In the simpler unweighted form (assuming $\mathbf{G}_1 = \mathbf{G}_2 = \mathbf{I}$, i.e., grasp points at object origin):

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{J}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

This is the **fundamental cooperative Jacobian** for a dual-arm system when both grasp points coincide with the object frame origin.

## 3.2 Derivation — Inverse Cooperative Kinematics (Pseudoinverse Solution)

### Step 1 — Set up the linear system

Given the desired object velocity $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o^{d}$:

$$
\mathbf{J}_o \dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dot{\mathbf{x}}_o^{d}
$$

Since the system is **kinematically redundant** ($n_1 + n_2 > 6$), the solution is not unique.

### Step 2 — Apply the pseudoinverse (minimum-norm solution)

The minimum-norm joint velocity is:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{*} = \mathbf{J}_o^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}}_o^{d} = \mathbf{J}_o^{T} (\mathbf{J}_o \mathbf{J}_o^{T})^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}_o^{d}
$$

### Step 3 — Add the null-space component for optimization

Any vector in the null space of $\mathbf{J}_o$ can be added without affecting the object motion:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}_o^{+} \dot{\mathbf{x}}_o^{d} + (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}_o^{+}\mathbf{J}_o)\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0
$$

The term $(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{J}_o^{+}\mathbf{J}_o)$ is the **null-space projector** $\mathbf{N}$. The arbitrary vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0$ is chosen to optimize a secondary criterion, e.g.:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}_0 = k \left(\frac{\partial w(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\right)^{T}
$$

where $w(\mathbf{q})$ is a scalar performance index (e.g., manipulability, distance from joint limits).

## 3.3 Derivation — Cooperative Force Relationship

### Step 1 — Apply the principle of virtual work

For a single robot, $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^{T} \mathbf{F}$ where $\mathbf{F}$ is the end-effector wrench. For the cooperative system, virtual work must balance across **all** robots and the object:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} \delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{F}_o^{T} \delta \mathbf{x}_o
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the velocity relationship

Using $\delta \mathbf{x}_o = \mathbf{J}_o \delta \mathbf{q}$, i.e., $\delta \mathbf{q} = \mathbf{J}_o^{+} \delta \mathbf{x}_o$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau}^{T} \mathbf{J}_o^{+} \delta \mathbf{x}_o = \mathbf{F}_o^{T} \delta \mathbf{x}_o
$$

### Step 3 — Identify the cooperative torque

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}_o^{T} \mathbf{F}_o}
$$

This shows that the cooperative Jacobian is the **dual mapping** for force transmission, exactly as in the single-arm case.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (Conceptual — for board-style clarity)**
> Two 2-DOF planar arms, each with Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_i$ of size $3 \times 2$ (planar: $v_x, v_y, \omega_z$). They grasp an object at $\mathbf{r}_{1/o} = [0.1, 0, 0]^{T}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{2/o} = [-0.1, 0, 0]^{T}$ relative to the object frame (in meters).
>
> At the current configuration, the individual Jacobians evaluate to:
> $$\mathbf{J}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 \\ 0.0 & 0.4 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{J}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.1 & 0.3 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> The grasp matrices are:
> $$\mathbf{G}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & -0.1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{G}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> In the simplified case where the grasp points coincide with the object origin ($\mathbf{G}_i = \mathbf{I}$), the cooperative Jacobian becomes:
> $$\mathbf{J}_o = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.4 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.4 & 0.1 & 0.3 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 & 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$
> This $3 \times 4$ matrix is rank-deficient in the planar case if the object rotation is decoupled — a common source of singularities in cooperative systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Cooperative Kinematics

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional flow of data/transformations in a cooperative kinematic solver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT Desired Object Trajectory xo of t"]
    B["MODULE 1 Inverse Cooperative Kinematics Solver"]
    C["OUTPUT Joint Trajectories q1 of t and q2 of t"]
    D["MODULE 2 Forward Cooperative Kinematics Validator"]
    E["VALIDATION Computed xo predicted vs xo desired"]
    F["MODULE 3 Cooperative Jacobian Jo Computation"]
    G["MODULE 4 Null Space Optimizer"]
    H["OUTPUT Optimized Null Space Velocity q0"]
    I["MODULE 5 Singularity and Internal Force Checker"]

    A --> B
    B --> F
    F --> B
    B --> C
    B --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B
    C --> D
    D --> E
    F --> I
    I --> B

    classDef io fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    classDef val fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000

    class A,C,H io
    class B,F core
    class G,I opt
    class D,E val
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Dual-Arm Cooperative Manipulation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["ROBOT 1 q1"]
    R2["ROBOT 2 q2"]
    O["COMMON RIGID OBJECT"]
    W["WORKSPACE xo"]
    F1["GRASP 1 r1 over o"]
    F2["GRASP 2 r2 over o"]

    R1 -- End Effector Pose xe1 --> F1
    R2 -- End Effector Pose xe2 --> F2
    F1 -- Rigid Contact --> O
    F2 -- Rigid Contact --> O
    O -- Net Object Pose --> W
    R1 -. Joint Velocities q1 dot .-> C["COOPERATIVE JACOBIAN Jo"]
    R2 -. Joint Velocities q2 dot .-> C
    C -- Object Velocity xo dot --> O

    classDef robot fill:#D9E1F2,stroke:#2E75B6,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#FFE699,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000
    classDef math fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#548235,color:#000
    classDef ws fill:#F4B084,stroke:#C55A11,color:#000

    class R1,R2 robot
    class O obj
    class C math
    class W,F1,F2 ws
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Hierarchy for Cooperative Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["WORLD FRAME W"]
    B["OBJECT FRAME O at COM"]
    E1["END EFFECTOR 1 FRAME E1"]
    E2["END EFFECTOR 2 FRAME E2"]
    B1["BASE FRAME 1 B1"]
    B2["BASE FRAME 2 B2"]
    J1["JOINT FRAMES 1 to n1"]
    J2["JOINT FRAMES 1 to n2"]

    W --> B1
    W --> B2
    B1 --> J1
    J1 --> E1
    B2 --> J2
    J2 --> E2
    W --> B
    E1 --> B
    E2 --> B

    classDef world fill:#DEEBF7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef obj fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#BF8F00,color:#000
    classDef arm fill:#E2EFDA,stroke:#548235,color:#000

    class W world
    class B obj
    class B1,B2,E1,E2,J1,J2 arm
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define cooperative kinematics and distinguish it from single-arm kinematics.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Cooperative kinematics is the study of motion in multi-robot systems that physically interact through a common task (e.g., a rigid object) or through direct contact. Unlike single-arm kinematics, where the end-effector pose depends only on one robot's joint vector $\mathbf{q}_1$, cooperative kinematics couples **multiple** joint vectors $\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{q}_n$ to a single **task-space** variable (the object pose $\mathbf{x}_o$) via geometric and constraint equations such as $\mathbf{x}_o = \mathbf{f}_o(\mathbf{q}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{q}_n)$. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2
**Q: What is the cooperative Jacobian? State its dimensions for a dual-arm 6-DOF system.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The cooperative Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_o$ is the matrix that maps the stacked joint velocities of all robots in a cooperative system to the velocity of the commonly manipulated object: $\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o = \mathbf{J}_o \dot{\mathbf{q}}$. For a dual-arm system where each arm has 6 revolute joints (i.e., $n_1 = n_2 = 6$), and the object has 6 DOF in space, the cooperative Jacobian has dimensions **$6 \times 12$**. The system is therefore redundant by 6 DOF, which can be used for null-space optimization. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q: (a)** Derive the cooperative object Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_o$ for a dual-arm system co-manipulating a rigid object. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Two 6-DOF arms grasp a rigid box at symmetric points. Given that the object must translate with velocity $\mathbf{v}_o = [0.1, 0, 0]^{T}$ m/s and rotate with $\boldsymbol{\omega}_o = [0, 0, 0.2]^{T}$ rad/s, write the cooperative velocity equation and identify the null-space dimension. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

We consider two robots with $n_1$ and $n_2$ revolute joints rigidly grasping a common object. Let $\mathbf{q} = [\mathbf{q}_1^T, \mathbf{q}_2^T]^T$ be the stacked joint vector, and $\mathbf{x}_o = [\mathbf{p}_o^T, \boldsymbol{\phi}_o^T]^T$ the object pose.

**Assumption 1 (Rigid grasp):** No relative motion occurs at the contact points. Therefore, the $i$-th end-effector velocity equals the velocity of the corresponding material point on the object:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o
$$
where $\mathbf{G}_i$ is the grasp matrix defined as $\mathbf{G}_i = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I} & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o}) \\ \mathbf{0} & \mathbf{I} \end{bmatrix}$.

**Assumption 2 (Standard DH):** Each robot's individual Jacobian $\mathbf{J}_i$ is computed using the standard Denavit-Hartenberg convention.

**Step 1:** Write the differential kinematics for each arm:
$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i)\dot{\mathbf{q}}_i
$$

**Step 2:** Equate the two expressions for $\mathbf{v}_i$:
$$
\mathbf{J}_i(\mathbf{q}_i)\dot{\mathbf{q}}_i = \mathbf{G}_i \mathbf{v}_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}_o = \mathbf{G}_i^{-1} \mathbf{J}_i \dot{\mathbf{q}}_i
$$

**Step 3:** Stack both equations and write the cooperative velocity relationship:
$$
\mathbf{v}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{G}_1^{-1}\mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Therefore, the cooperative Jacobian is:
$$
\boxed{\mathbf{J}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{G}_1^{-1}\mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

If both grasp points coincide with the object origin ($\mathbf{G}_i = \mathbf{I}$), this simplifies to $\mathbf{J}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** Stacked object velocity:
$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_o \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(units: m/s and rad/s)}
$$

**Step 2:** Cooperative velocity equation:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 0.1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{J}_o \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\mathbf{q}}_1 \\ \dot{\mathbf{q}}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$
where $\mathbf{J}_o$ is a $6 \times 12$ matrix.

**Step 3:** Null-space dimension:
$$
\dim(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J}_o)) = 12 - \text{rank}(\mathbf{J}_o)
$$
For a generic (non-singular) configuration with full-rank cooperative Jacobian, rank$(\mathbf{J}_o) = 6$, so:
$$
\dim(\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{J}_o)) = 12 - 6 = \mathbf{6}
$$

**Interpretation:** 6 internal DOFs can be used to optimize secondary objectives like joint-limit avoidance, manipulability maximization, or internal force control (squeezing the object). **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**Q: (a)** Explain the rigid grasp constraint in cooperative robotics. Derive the relationship between end-effector velocities and object velocity. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** Two planar 2-DOF arms grasp a rigid rod. Arm 1 has joints $\mathbf{q}_1 = [q_{11}, q_{12}]^T$ and Arm 2 has $\mathbf{q}_2 = [q_{21}, q_{22}]^T$. The individual Jacobians at the current configuration are:
$$
\mathbf{J}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{J}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$
Compute the cooperative Jacobian (assuming grasp points coincide with object origin) and verify its rank. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **rigid grasp constraint** states that when a robot end-effector is rigidly attached to a point on a rigid object, the velocity of the end-effector must equal the velocity of that material point on the object. Mathematically, for the $i$-th grasp point at position $\mathbf{r}_{i/o}$ from the object center:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{v}_o + \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \times \mathbf{r}_{i/o}
$$

In 6-vector form, the linear velocity $\mathbf{v}_i$ and angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ of the end-effector are related to the object's twist $\mathbf{v}_o = [\mathbf{v}_o^T, \boldsymbol{\omega}_o^T]^T$ via the **grasp matrix** $\mathbf{G}_i$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_i \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{I}_3 & -\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o}) \\ \mathbf{0}_3 & \mathbf{I}_3 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{G}_i} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v}_o \\ \boldsymbol{\omega}_o \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o})$ is the skew-symmetric matrix of $\mathbf{r}_{i/o}$. This constraint is the **physical coupling** that makes the two arms behave as one cooperative system. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1:** With $\mathbf{G}_1 = \mathbf{G}_2 = \mathbf{I}_3$ (grasp points at object origin), the cooperative Jacobian is the horizontal stack of the two individual Jacobians:

$$
\mathbf{J}_o = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{J}_1 & \mathbf{J}_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 & 0.4 & 0.3 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 & 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a $3 \times 4$ matrix.

**Step 2:** Compute the rank. Notice that the third row is the **sum of the first row of $\mathbf{J}_1$ extended and the first row of $\mathbf{J}_2$ extended** — specifically, row 3 = row 1 of $\mathbf{J}_1$ (when considering $q_{11}$ and $q_{12}$) summed with row 1 of $\mathbf{J}_2$ (when considering $q_{21}$ and $q_{22}$) only if we re-arrange. Let us check linear dependence directly:

The third row is $[1, 1, 1, 1]$.
The first row is $[0.6, 0.2, 0.4, 0.3]$.
The second row is $[0.0, 0.5, 0.2, 0.4]$.

No scalar multiple of row 1 or row 2 equals row 3, and row 1 and row 2 are clearly independent. However, we must check whether all three rows together have rank 3.

Determinant test (for $3\times 3$ submatrices): Consider the submatrix formed by columns 1, 2, 3:

$$
\det \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 & 0.4 \\ 0.0 & 0.5 & 0.2 \\ 1.0 & 1.0 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} = 0.6(0.5 \cdot 1.0 - 0.2 \cdot 1.0) - 0.2(0.0 - 0.2) + 0.4(0.0 - 0.5)
$$
$$
= 0.6(0.3) - 0.2(-0.2) + 0.4(-0.5) = 0.18 + 0.04 - 0.20 = 0.02 \neq 0
$$

**Step 3:** Since a $3\times 3$ submatrix has non-zero determinant:
$$
\boxed{\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}_o) = 3}
$$

**Step 4:** Null-space dimension = $4 - 3 = 1$. The cooperative planar system has 1 internal DOF that does not affect the object motion — a common scenario in planar dual-arm manipulation that restricts internal reconfiguration. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing the cooperative Jacobian with stacked individual Jacobians without grasp correction.** Students frequently write $\mathbf{J}_o = [\mathbf{J}_1, \mathbf{J}_2]$ even when grasp points do **not** coincide with the object origin. The correct form includes the grasp matrix: $\mathbf{J}_o = [\mathbf{G}_1^{-1}\mathbf{J}_1, \mathbf{G}_2^{-1}\mathbf{J}_2]$. Loss: up to **2 marks per sub-part**.
> 2. **Forgetting the skew-symmetric cross-product term** when $\mathbf{r}_{i/o} \neq \mathbf{0}$. The term $-\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o})\boldsymbol{\omega}_o$ is the velocity contribution due to object rotation about its center — omitting it makes the constraint equation **physically wrong**. Loss: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Assuming invertibility of $\mathbf{J}_o$ in redundant systems.** Since $\mathbf{J}_o$ is rectangular (tall or wide), it cannot be directly inverted. Students must use the **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}_o^{+}$ or the **damped least-squares** form. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Sign errors in the cross-product matrix.** $\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{a})\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}$, so $-\mathbf{S}(\mathbf{r}_{i/o})\boldsymbol{\omega}_o$ is correct, but $-\mathbf{S}(\boldsymbol{\omega}_o)\mathbf{r}_{i/o}$ is also valid (since $\mathbf{a}\times\mathbf{b} = -\mathbf{b}\times\mathbf{a}$). Do not mix both signs. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Singularity oversight.** When the cooperative Jacobian loses rank, the system cannot achieve certain object motions. Students must always verify $\det(\mathbf{J}_o\mathbf{J}_o^T) > 0$ (manipulability test). Loss: **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cooperative kinematics** = joint-space to shared task-space mapping for multi-robot systems with rigid coupling.
- **Cooperative Jacobian** $\mathbf{J}_o$ is the central object — it is built from individual Jacobians **premultiplied** by the inverse grasp matrices.
