> - Arrows flowing into the **Coupling Compensator (stepF)** from both robots illustrate the *bidirectional* nature of dynamic coupling.
> - The **Force/Torque Sensors (stepL)** are essential in practice because the coupling forces cannot be computed analytically with 100% accuracy — sensor feedback closes the loop.
> - The **Common Object (stepK)** is the *physical* manifestation of the coupling — it is the medium through which one robot "feels" the other.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Dynamic Coupling in Cooperative Robotics
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic Coupling is the mutual dynamic interaction between two or more robots operating in a cooperative task, where the motion and forces of one robot directly influence the other through shared constraints (e.g., a common object). It is mathematically represented by the off-diagonal blocks $M_{ij}$ ($i \neq j$) in the composite inertia matrix of the multi-robot system. The coupling arises from (a) kinematic constraints imposed by the common object, (b) force transmission through the object, and (c) inertial effects propagating through the closed kinematic chain.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark mathematical form + 1 mark three sources)**

---

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Internal Forces and External Forces in Cooperative Manipulation
> `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Internal Force $F_{int}$ | External Force $F_{ext}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Force exerted by one robot on the object that is balanced by an equal and opposite force from another robot | Force from the environment acting on the object |
| Effect on object | Cancels out in the force balance equation; does not produce object motion if balanced | Produces net acceleration of the object |
| Mathematical role | Lies in the null space of grasp matrix $G$ | Lies in the range of grasp matrix $G$ |
| Control concern | Must be controlled to prevent object slippage or excessive squeeze | Must be controlled to track desired trajectory |
| Example | Squeezing force between two grippers | Weight of payload, contact force from ground |

**(3 marks: 2 marks for distinction table + 1 mark for example)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question A: Coupled Dynamics of Dual-Arm Cooperative Manipulation
> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the coupled equation of motion for two $n$-DOF robots cooperatively manipulating a rigid object using the Lagrangian formulation. Clearly identify the coupling inertia matrix terms.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define System Variables (1 mark)**
- Robot 1 has $n$ joints, configuration $q_1 \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- Robot 2 has $n$ joints, configuration $q_2 \in \mathbb{R}^n$
- Object pose: $x_o \in \mathbb{R}^6$
- Object mass: $m_o$, inertia: $I_o$

**Step 2: Constraint Equation (1 mark)**
The kinematic closure at the grasp points gives:
$$f_1(q_1) = f_2(q_2) = x_o$$
Differentiating: $J_1(q_1)\dot{q}_1 = J_2(q_2)\dot{q}_2$

**Step 3: Kinetic Energy (2 marks)**
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}_1^T M_1(q_1)\dot{q}_1 + \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}_2^T M_2(q_2)\dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\dot{x}_o^T M_o \dot{x}_o$$
Substituting $\dot{x}_o = J_1\dot{q}_1$:
$$T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \begin{bmatrix} M_1 + J_1^T M_o J_1 & 0 \\ 0 & M_2 \end{bmatrix}\dot{q}$$

**Step 4: Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation (2 marks)**
After constraint reduction (as derived in Section 3.1):
$$M_{reduced}(q)\ddot{q}_r + C_{reduced}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_r + G_{reduced}(q) = \tau_r$$
where $M_{reduced}$ contains the coupling terms.

**Step 5: Identify Coupling Blocks (1 mark)**
The coupling terms appear as $J_2^T M_o J_2$ in the reduced inertia matrix — physically representing how the object (held by both) creates a dynamic link between robots.

---

**(b) Two 2-DOF planar robots cooperatively lift a rigid bar of mass $m_o = 5$ kg and length $L = 1$ m. At a given instant, the Jacobians are $J_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$. The robot inertias are $M_1 = M_2 = 2 I_{2\times 2}$ kg·m². Compute the coupled reduced inertia matrix. What is the coupling inertia term?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Object Mass Matrix (1 mark)**
For a 2D planar rigid bar: $M_o = \begin{bmatrix} m_o & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & m_o & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & I_o \end{bmatrix}$
With $m_o = 5$ kg and $I_o = \frac{1}{12}m_o L^2 = \frac{1}{12}(5)(1)^2 = 0.4167$ kg·m²:

$$M_o = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.4167 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Compute Coupling Inertia $J_2^T M_o J_2$ (2 marks)**
For 2D planar case (2 × 2 Jacobian, restricted to 2D object dynamics $m_o I_{2\times 2}$):
$$J_2^T M_o J_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute Self-Coupling Term $J_2^T J_1^{-T} M_1 J_1^{-1} J_2$ (2 marks)**
Since $J_1 = J_2 = I_{2\times 2}$:
$$J_2^T J_1^{-T} M_1 J_1^{-1} J_2 = M_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Reduced Inertia (1 mark)**
$$M_{reduced} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 0 \\ 0 & 9 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Identify Coupling Term (1 mark)**
The **coupling inertia term is $J_2^T M_o J_2 = 5 I_{2\times 2}$ kg·m²** — this represents the inertial "link" between the two robots mediated by the common object. Without the object, $M_{coupling} = 0$ and the robots would be dynamically independent.

---

### Question B: Force Decomposition and Internal Force Control in Cooperative Systems
> `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Explain the force decomposition principle in cooperative manipulation. How are internal and external forces separated mathematically? Derive the condition for internal force control.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Grasp Matrix Definition (1 mark)**
For $k$ robots grasping an object, the grasp matrix $G \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times 6k}$ maps individual end-effector wrenches $F_{hi} \in \mathbb{R}^6$ to the object wrench $F_o \in \mathbb{R}^6$:
$$F_o = \sum_{i=1}^{k} G_i^T F_{hi}$$

**Step 2: Force Decomposition (2 marks)**
The space of end-effector wrenches $F_h = [F_{h1}^T, \ldots, F_{hk}^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{6k}$ decomposes into two orthogonal subspaces:
- **Range of $G^T$:** produces object motion (external forces)
- **Null space of $G$:** produces internal squeezing/squeezing forces

**Step 3: Mathematical Decomposition (2 marks)**
$$F_h = F_{ext} + F_{int}$$
where:
$$F_{ext} = G^{+T} F_o \quad (\text{force component producing motion})$$
$$F_{int} = N(G) \lambda \quad (\text{internal force, where } N(G)\lambda \in \ker(G))$$

**Step 4: Internal Force Control Condition (2 marks)**
To control internal forces without affecting object motion:
$$\lambda = (G N(G))^{+}(F_{int,d} - F_{int})$$
where $F_{int,d}$ is the desired internal force and $F_{int}$ is the measured internal force. The condition $G \cdot N(G) = 0$ ensures that internal forces produce **zero net object wrench** — they only squeeze or stretch the object.

---

**(b) A dual-arm robot performs a cooperative insertion task. Robot 1 exerts a wrench $F_{h1} = [10, 5, 0, 0, 0, 2]^T$ N/N·m and Robot 2 exerts $F_{h2} = [-10, -3, 0, 0, 0, -2]^T$ N/N·m on a peg. Identify the external and internal wrench components. State whether the grasp is in equilibrium and whether internal forces could damage the peg.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Total Wrench on Object (1 mark)**
$$F_o = F_{h1} + F_{h2} = \begin{bmatrix} 10-10 \\ 5-3 \\ 0+0 \\ 0+0 \\ 0+0 \\ 2-2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Identify External Wrench (1 mark)**
$$F_{ext} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 2 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/N·m}$$
A pure 2 N force in the y-direction — this produces **peg motion**.

**Step 3: Identify Internal Wrench (2 marks)**
$$F_{int} = F_{h1} - F_{h2} = \begin{bmatrix} 10-(-10) \\ 5-(-3) \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 2-(-2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 8 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 0 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/N·m}$$

**Step 4: Equilibrium Check (1 mark)**
Check force balance: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 2$ N, $\sum M_z = 0$
The system is **NOT in full equilibrium** because there is a net 2 N force in the y-direction. However, force balance in x is maintained ($10 + (-10) = 0$), and moment balance is maintained.

**Step 5: Damage Assessment (2 marks)**
The internal force magnitude $\vert F_{int} \vert = \sqrt{20^2 + 8^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{400 + 64 + 16} = \sqrt{480} \approx 21.9$ N is significant. **Risk Assessment:**
- If the peg material fragility threshold is, say, **15 N** of internal squeeze, the peg could **fracture**.
- **Recommendation:** Reduce $F_{h1,x}$ and $F_{h2,x}$ to limit internal squeeze; the moment component of 4 N·m is also non-trivial.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dynamic Coupling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Object's Inertia (–2 marks):** Students often write only $M_1$ and $M_2$ in the inertia matrix and forget $J^T M_o J$ — the object is the *physical reason* for coupling!
>
> 2. **Treating Robots as Independent (–3 marks):** Never write the dynamics of robot 1 *without* including its dependence on $q_2$. Every cooperative question demands the **composite** equation.
>
> 3. **Confusing Force and Wrench Transformation (–2 marks):** $J^T$ (transpose) maps Cartesian force to joint torque, NOT $J^{-1}$. Memorize: $\tau = J^T F$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring Internal Forces in Force Decomposition (–2 marks):** When asked about "forces in cooperative manipulation," students often write only $F_{ext}$ and lose 2 marks for forgetting $F_{int}$.
>
> 5. **Missing Units in Numerical Problems (–1 mark):** Always carry units through every step. KTU examiners explicitly deduct for unit-less final answers.
>
> 6. **Not Justifying the Symmetry of $M(q)$ (–1 mark):** $M(q)$ is always symmetric positive definite — state this property explicitly to gain an extra mark for "rigor."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Dynamic Coupling** = mutual dynamic influence between cooperating robots via shared object/constraints
- [x] **Composite inertia matrix** $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{2n \times 2n}$ has off-diagonal coupling blocks $M_{ij} \neq 0$ when $i \neq j$
- [x] **Kinematic constraint:** $J_1\dot{q}_1 = J_2\dot{q}_2$ — velocities are locked through the object
- [x] **Force decomposition:** $F_h = F_{ext} + F_{int}$; internal forces lie in null space of grasp matrix
- [x] **Coupling inertia formula:** $M_{ij} = J_i^T M_o J_j$ — physically links robots through the object
- [x] **Equations of motion form:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau + J^T F$
- [x] **Euler-Lagrange** formulation produces the same result as Newton-Euler for cooperative systems
- [x] **Constraint reduction** reduces $2n$ equations to $n$ effective equations
- [x] **Internal force control** must lie in $\ker(G)$ to avoid producing object motion
- [x] **Symmetry and positive-definiteness** of $M(q)$ are guaranteed structural properties

### 📌 Three Must-Memorize Equations

1. **Composite coupled equation of motion:**
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau + J^T F$$

2. **Velocity constraint at common object:**
$$J_1(q_1)\dot{q}_1 = J_2(q_2)\dot{q}_2$$

3. **Force balance on cooperatively manipulated object:**
$$F_o = \sum_{i=1}^{k} G_i^T F_{hi}, \quad F_{int} = N(G)\lambda$$

### 🎯 Common KTU Question Patterns

| Pattern | Likely Marks | Key Topic |
|---|---|---|
| "Derive coupled dynamics for two robots..." | 7–14 | Lagrangian / Newton-Euler |
| "What is dynamic coupling? Explain sources." | 3–7 | Conceptual + Kinematic/Force/Inertial sources |
| "Compute reduced inertia matrix for given Jacobians..." | 7 | Numerical evaluation |
| "Explain internal vs external force decomposition..." | 7 | Force analysis |
| "Design a coupling compensator for..." | 10–14 | Control systems integration |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When you see "cooperative" or "dual-arm" in any KTU question, *immediately* think of the composite inertia matrix with off-diagonal coupling blocks. This single concept unlocks 70% of cooperative robotics exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Approach to Distributed Cooperative Control Problems with an Optimization Objective: Cooperation Constraints and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Approach to Distributed Cooperative Control Problems with an Optimization Objective: Cooperation Constraints and Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distributed Cooperative Control with an Optimization Objective** is the formulation of multi-agent coordination as a *mathematical optimization problem* in which (i) each agent computes its own control action using only locally available information, (ii) the *cooperation constraints* couple the agents through a shared information/communication graph, and (iii) the *cooperation objective* is a global cost function that the team seeks to minimize (or maximize) collectively.

Formally, for a network of $N$ agents indexed by $i \in \mathcal{V} = \{1, 2, \ldots, N\}$, the cooperative problem is stated as:

$$\min_{u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_N}\ \ J(x) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i, u_i) \;+\; f_c(x_1, \ldots, x_N)$$

subject to:

$$g_i(x_i, u_i) \le 0,\quad h_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \le 0,\quad x_i \text{ known only to } i \text{ and } \mathcal{N}_i$$

where $f_i$ is the **local cost**, $f_c$ is the **cooperation/coupling cost**, $g_i$ are **local constraints** (actuator limits, safety), $h_{ij}$ are **cooperation constraints** (collision avoidance, formation, connectivity), and $\mathcal{N}_i$ is the neighbor set defined by the communication graph $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Rowing Crew

Imagine an **eight-person rowing shell** crossing a finish line. Each rower (agent) has:
- A *local* goal — to keep their own stroke rate steady (local objective $f_i$).
- A *physical* constraint — their oar must not collide with the neighboring rower (cooperation constraint $h_{ij}$).
- A *team* objective — the boat must reach the finish line as fast as possible, which depends on the synchronization of **all** rowers (global $J$).

No rower can see the entire boat — they only feel the rower in front and behind. Yet by sensing neighbors and adjusting their stroke (a *local* control decision), the crew minimizes the total race time (the *global* objective). That is precisely **distributed cooperative control with an optimization objective**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooperation Constraints** — restrictions that *bind* agents to one another (collision avoidance, network connectivity, formation geometry, shared resource limits).
>
> **Cooperation Objectives** — the team-wide performance metric (consensus error, formation potential, coverage utility, rendezvous time).
>
> **Distributed** — each agent's control law depends only on its *own* state and the states of its *immediate neighbors*; no central processor is assumed.

The control designer must:
1. Translate the engineering task into an **objective function** $J$.
2. Express the *coupling* between agents as **constraints**.
3. Devise a **decomposable** algorithm so each agent can solve a local problem whose solution also solves the global problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Three-Layer Architecture of the Formulation

A KTU-standard formulation of distributed cooperative control has three logical layers. Every exam answer should reference this scaffolding.

### Layer 1 — The Team Objective (What the team wants)

The global cost is **separable + coupling**:

$$J(x) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i)}_{\text{local cost (separable)}} \;+\; \underbrace{f_c(x_1,\dots,x_N)}_{\text{coupling cost (team-wide)}}$$

- *Separable* means each $f_i$ depends only on agent $i$ — agents can compute it locally.
- *Coupling* $f_c$ depends on multiple agents — this is the **cooperation objective**.

### Layer 2 — Cooperation Constraints (How the team is bound)

Two families of constraints appear:

$$h_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \le 0, \quad \forall (i,j) \in \mathcal{E}$$

- **Pair-wise geometric constraints**: $\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \ge d_{safe}$ (collision), $\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert = d_{ij}^{*}$ (formation).
- **Graph-theoretic constraints**: the communication Laplacian $L$ must remain connected (no isolated agents).

### Layer 3 — Distributed Information Structure (Who knows what)

Agent $i$ can use only the information contained in its **neighborhood** $\mathcal{N}_i \cup \{i\}$. Mathematically, the control input $u_i$ is a function:

$$u_i \;=\; \kappa_i\bigl(\,x_i,\ \{x_j\}_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}\,\bigr)$$

This is the **distributed information constraint**, and it is what distinguishes cooperative control from centralized optimal control.

## 2.2 Classical Cooperation Objectives in Engineering Practice

| Objective | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Consensus** | $\min \sum_{i<j} a_{ij}\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$ | All agents converge to a common value |
| **Formation** | $\min \sum_{i<j} a_{ij}\Vert (x_i - x_j) - d_{ij}^{*}\Vert^2$ | Maintain prescribed inter-agent distances |
| **Coverage** | $\min \sum_{q \in Q} \min_i \Vert q - x_i\Vert^2$ | Spread agents to monitor region $Q$ |
| **Rendezvous** | $\min \sum_i \Vert x_i - x_g\Vert^2$ | Meet at goal $x_g$ |
| **Task Allocation** | $\min \sum_i \sum_j c_{ij} \cdot y_{ij}$ | Minimize total assignment cost |
| **Energy / Fuel** | $\min \sum_i \int \Vert u_i(t)\Vert^2\, dt$ | Cooperative energy-optimal motion |

## 2.3 Cooperation Constraints in Engineering Practice

| Constraint | Mathematical Form | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Collision avoidance** | $\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert \ge d_{safe}$ | Robots/vehicles must keep a safety bubble |
| **Connectivity** | $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ | The communication graph stays connected |
| **Actuator saturation** | $u_i \in \mathcal{U}_i$ | Each robot's motors have bounded torque |
| **Velocity bounds** | $\Vert \dot{x}_i\Vert \le v_{max}$ | Physical speed limits of mobile robots |
| **Formation rigidity** | $\text{rank}(R) = 2N - 3$ | Distance constraints define a rigid shape |
| **Bearing-only** | $\measuredangle(x_i - x_j) = \theta_{ij}^{*}$ | Vision sensors constrain directions, not distances |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \sum_i f_i + f_c$ | Global cooperative objective | Problem statement |
| 2 | $\nabla J = \sum_i \nabla_{x_i} J$ | Separable gradient | Decomposition proof |
| 3 | $L = J + \sum_i \lambda_i g_i + \sum_{i,j} \mu_{ij} h_{ij}$ | Lagrangian with constraints | Dual decomposition |
| 4 | $x_i^{k+1} = x_i^k - \alpha \nabla_{x_i} f_i$ | Local gradient step | Distributed optimization |
| 5 | $x_i^{k+1} = x_i^k - \alpha \nabla_{x_i} f_i - \beta \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(x_i^k - x_j^k)$ | Consensus + gradient | Cooperative optimization |
| 6 | $L = D - A$ (graph Laplacian) | Encodes the network | Connectivity / consensus |
| 7 | $\dot{x} = -L x$ | Continuous-time consensus | Formation analysis |
| 8 | $\min c^{\top}x \;\; s.t.\;\; Ax = b,\; x \ge 0$ | Standard LP form | Task allocation |
| 9 | $K^* = \arg\min_K \sum_i J_i(K)$ | Coupled Riccati search | Cooperative LQR |
| 10 | $h_{ij}(x_i,x_j) \le 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mu_{ij} \ge 0$ | KKT sign condition | Constraint handling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember for KTU**: $\nabla_{x_i} f_c$ is *not* computable by agent $i$ alone because $f_c$ depends on other agents' states. This is exactly why the **distributed** algorithm must use *neighbor information* to approximate $\nabla_{x_i} f_c$ — that is the heart of distributed cooperative optimization.

## 2.5 Why Optimization Formulation? (The Engineering "Why")

Optimization is preferred over ad-hoc control laws because it gives:
1. **Provable optimality** — KKT conditions guarantee the team reaches the best possible trade-off.
2. **Constraint handling** — physical, safety, and communication limits can be enforced systematically.
3. **Scalability** — distributed solvers (ADMM, dual decomposition) scale to thousands of agents.
4. **Reconfigurability** — change the objective and the same algorithm solves a new cooperative task.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — From the Global Problem to the Distributed Update Law

We start from the global cooperative optimization and show how a *local* iterative law at each agent achieves the *global* optimum. This derivation is a favourite **14-mark** KTU question.

### Step 1 — State the Global Cooperative Problem

Consider $N$ agents, each with decision variable $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$, communicating on graph $\mathcal{G}$.

$$\min_{x_1,\ldots,x_N}\ \ \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j)$$

Here $f_i$ is the *local* (separable) cost and $\phi_{ij}$ is the *pair-wise coupling* cost — the cooperation objective.

### Step 2 — Introduce the Consensus / Coupling Reformulation

To expose the coupling structure, introduce auxiliary copies $z_{ij}$ that must agree:

$$\min_{x_i, z_{ij}} \ \sum_i f_i(x_i) + \sum_{(i,j)} \phi_{ij}(z_{ij}) \quad s.t. \quad z_{ij} = z_{ji} = x_i$$

The equality constraint $z_{ij} = x_i$ encodes *information sharing* between neighbors.

### Step 3 — Form the Augmented Lagrangian

Add the consensus constraints with multipliers $\lambda_{ij}$ and a quadratic penalty $\rho \gt 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_\rho(x, z, \lambda) \;=\;& \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} \phi_{ij}(z_{ij}) \\
&+\; \sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} \lambda_{ij}^{\top}(x_i - z_{ij}) \;+\; \frac{\rho}{2}\sum_{(i,j)}\Vert x_i - z_{ij}\Vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic*: We replaced the hard equality $x_i = z_{ij}$ by a Lagrangian term $\lambda_{ij}^{\top}(x_i - z_{ij})$ and added a quadratic penalty to make the problem strictly convex (so it can be solved by simple gradient steps).

### Step 4 — Decompose into Agent-Local Sub-Problems

For each agent $i$, group the terms that involve $x_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\rho,i}(x_i) \;=\; f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \Bigl[\lambda_{ij}^{\top} x_i \;+\; \tfrac{\rho}{2}\Vert x_i - z_{ij}\Vert^2\Bigr]$$

This expression depends *only* on $x_i$, on $z_{ij}$ for $j \in \mathcal{N}_i$, and on the multiplier $\lambda_{ij}$ — quantities that agent $i$ knows or receives from its neighbors. Therefore agent $i$ can minimize it locally.

### Step 5 — Take the Gradient and Write the Distributed Update

$$\nabla_{x_i} \mathcal{L}_{\rho,i} \;=\; \nabla f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}\bigl[\lambda_{ij} \;+\; \rho(x_i - z_{ij})\bigr]$$

Setting the gradient to zero and using a step-size $\alpha = 1/\rho$ gives the **distributed update law**:

$$
\boxed{\,x_i^{k+1} \;=\; x_i^{k} \;-\; \alpha\,\nabla f_i(x_i^{k}) \;-\; \beta\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}\bigl(x_i^{k} - x_j^{k}\bigr)\,}
$$

> *Conversion logic*: When $z_{ij} \approx x_j$ (the auxiliary variable mirrors the neighbor's state), the term $\rho(x_i - z_{ij})$ reduces to $\rho(x_i - x_j)$. With $\beta = \rho$, the second term becomes a *consensus* coupling between neighbors — exactly the distributed cooperative update.

### Step 6 — Convergence Condition

The update converges to the global optimum of $J$ provided:
- Each $f_i$ is **strongly convex** with constant $m > 0$ and has **Lipschitz gradient** with constant $L$.
- The step-size satisfies: $0 < \alpha < 2/L$.
- The graph $\mathcal{G}$ is **connected** ($\lambda_2(L) > 0$).

## 3.2 Derivation B — Cooperative Objective as a Quadratic Form

A frequently asked KTU derivation: show that the *consensus objective* reduces to $x^{\top} L x$.

### Step 1 — Write the Pair-Wise Cooperation Objective

$$J_c(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} a_{ij}\,\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Squared Norm

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_c(x) \;=\;& \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\,(x_i^{\top}x_i + x_j^{\top}x_j - 2x_i^{\top}x_j) \\
=\;& \frac{1}{2}\Bigl[\sum_i d_i\, x_i^{\top}x_i \;+\; \sum_i d_i\, x_i^{\top}x_i \;-\; 2\sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}x_i^{\top}x_j\Bigr] \\
=\;& \sum_i d_i\, x_i^{\top}x_i \;-\; \sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\, x_i^{\top}x_j
\end{aligned}$$

> *Conversion logic*: The first sum counts $x_i^{\top}x_i$ twice (once from edge $(i,j)$ and once from $(j,i)$), hence the factor $2$ in the first line collapses to $d_i$ in the second.

### Step 3 — Recognize the Laplacian

Using $L = D - A$ where $D = \mathrm{diag}(d_1,\dots,d_N)$ and $A = [a_{ij}]$:

$$
\boxed{\,J_c(x) \;=\; x^{\top} L \, x\,}
$$

> *Conversion logic*: The quadratic form $x^{\top} L x$ automatically counts each pair-wise distance cost once, weighted by the adjacency coefficient $a_{ij}$. This identity is the cornerstone of all consensus and formation proofs.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem** — Three robots form a triangle. The cooperation objective is to minimize:

$$J = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[(x_1-x_2)^2 + (x_2-x_3)^2 + (x_3-x_1)^2\bigr]$$

Starting from $x^{(0)} = (5, 1, -3)^{\top}$ with unit weights and step size $\alpha = 0.4$, find $x^{(1)}$ using the distributed update.

### Step 1 — Build the Adjacency and Laplacian

Each edge has weight 1, so:

$$L \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Gradient of J

$$\nabla J \;=\; L\, x$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Distributed Update $x^{k+1} = x^k - \alpha \nabla J$

$$
\begin{aligned}
L x^{(0)} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 10 - 1 + 3 \\ -5 + 2 + 3 \\ -5 - 1 - 6 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 0 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the New State

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^{(1)} \;=\; x^{(0)} - 0.4 \cdot L x^{(0)} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 5 \\ 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix} - 0.4 \begin{pmatrix} 12 \\ 0 \\ -12 \end{pmatrix} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} 0.2 \\ 1.0 \\ 1.8 \end{pmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic*: The update pushes $x_1$ toward the average of its neighbors, and likewise for $x_2, x_3$. As $k \to \infty$, all three values converge to the team average $(5+1-3)/3 = 1$, which is the unique minimizer of $J$ — proving that the *distributed* update law drives the team to the *global* cooperative optimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Distributed Cooperative Optimization Loop

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the flow inside *one* agent (say agent $i$) and how it communicates with its neighbors during one optimization iteration. It is the canonical mental picture examiners expect in 7-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State Sensor of Agent i] --> B[Local Cost f_i x_i]
    A --> C[Cooperation Constraint h_ij]
    B --> D[Local Gradient nabla f_i]
    C --> E[Constraint Residual]
    D --> F[Optimizer Block of Agent i]
    E --> F
    G[Neighbor j State via Comm Link] --> H[Consensus Coupling Sum]
    K[Neighbor k State via Comm Link] --> H
    H --> F
    F --> I[Update Law x_i new]
    I --> J[Actuator Command u_i]
    J --> L[Robot i Dynamics]
    L --> A
    I --> M[Broadcast to Neighbors]
    M --> G
    M --> K
    classDef sensor fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef compute fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef comm fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef plant fill:#fae8ff,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#581c87
    class A sensor
    class B,C,D,E,F,H,I compute
    class G,K,M comm
    class J,L plant
```

### Reading the Diagram (KTU Exam Tip)

1. The **left branch** computes the *local* gradient $\nabla f_i$ — a quantity agent $i$ can find alone.
2. The **right branch** receives neighbor states through the **communication layer** and forms the consensus-coupling sum $\sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}(x_i - x_j)$.
3. Both feed the **Optimizer Block**, which executes the update law:

$$x_i^{k+1} = x_i^k - \alpha \nabla f_i - \beta \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(x_i^k - x_j^k)$$

4. The new state is **broadcast** to neighbors, closing the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the exam**: A 7-mark "explain" answer that includes a labelled block diagram plus one equation per block almost always secures full marks, because it demonstrates both *architecture* and *mathematics*.

## 4.2 Cooperation Graph — A Visual Map of Constraints and Objectives

The following diagram shows how *cooperation constraints* and *cooperation objectives* interconnect through the communication graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph O[Cooperation Objective Layer]
        O1[Consensus Energy xT L x]
        O2[Formation Energy sum of d_ij errors]
        O3[Coverage Utility]
    end
    subgraph C[Cooperation Constraint Layer]
        C1[Collision Avoidance h_ij less than 0]
        C2[Connectivity lambda 2 L greater than 0]
        C3[Actuator Saturation u in U]
    end
    subgraph D[Distributed Information Layer]
        D1[Agent i knows only x_i and x_j for j in N_i]
    end
    O1 --> F[Feasible Team State x star]
    O2 --> F
    O3 --> F
    C1 --> F
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    D --> F
    classDef obj fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a
    classDef con fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef info fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#14532d
    classDef out fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#713f12
    class O1,O2,O3 obj
    class C1,C2,C3 con
    class D1 info
    class F out
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **blue layer** is what the team wants to achieve (objectives).
- The **red layer** is what limits how the team can achieve it (constraints).
- The **green layer** is the *information restriction* that forces the solution to be *distributed*.
- All three converge on the **feasible team state** $x^*$ — the optimum that satisfies every cooperation constraint *and* every cooperation objective.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define "cooperation constraints" in a multi-agent cooperative control problem. State two examples with their mathematical form.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Cooperation constraints are the restrictions that *couple* the decision variables of two or more agents and encode the rules the team must obey while achieving its joint objective. They appear in the optimization problem as inequality or equality functions of multiple agents' states.

**Example 1 — Collision avoidance (inequality constraint):**
$$\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \ge d_{safe},\quad \forall (i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$$
This ensures that the Euclidean distance between any pair of communicating agents never falls below a safety threshold $d_{safe}$.

**Example 2 — Connectivity (graph-theoretic constraint):**
$$\lambda_2(L(\mathcal{G})) \;\ge\; \lambda_{\min} \;>\; 0$$
The algebraic connectivity of the communication Laplacian must remain strictly positive, guaranteeing that the information graph stays connected throughout the mission.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — first example with equation; 1 mark — second example with equation.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between "cooperation objective" and "local objective" in distributed cooperative control. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **local objective** $f_i(x_i)$ depends only on the state (or control) of a *single* agent and can be computed by that agent alone — it represents an individual's private performance metric, such as its own energy consumption or path length.

A **cooperation objective** $f_c(x_1, \dots, x_N)$ depends on the states of *multiple* agents simultaneously and quantifies the *team-wide* performance, such as consensus error, formation fidelity, or collective coverage utility.

| Feature | Local Objective | Cooperation Objective |
|---|---|---|
| Depends on | Single agent $x_i$ | Multiple agents $x_1, \dots, x_N$ |
| Computable locally? | Yes | No (needs neighbor information) |
| Example | $f_i(x_i) = \Vert x_i - x_g\Vert^2$ | $f_c = \sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$ |

*(1 mark — local definition + example; 1 mark — cooperation definition + example; 1 mark — comparison table.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: In 14-mark questions on this topic, students *most commonly lose marks* in three places: (1) failing to write the **explicit constraint set** with proper set-builder notation, (2) skipping the **convergence condition** (connected graph + step-size bounds), and (3) presenting only the final update law without showing the Lagrangian step that leads to it. Always write the Lagrangian; it is worth 2–3 marks by itself.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Formulate the general distributed cooperative control optimization problem for $N$ agents communicating on graph $\mathcal{G}$. Clearly identify the cooperation objective and two cooperation constraints. (7 marks)**

**(b) Starting from this formulation, derive the distributed gradient + consensus update law using Lagrangian dual decomposition. State the convergence condition. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the multi-agent setting (1 mark)**

Let $\mathcal{G} = (\mathcal{V}, \mathcal{E})$ be the undirected communication graph with $N$ agents. Each agent $i$ has decision variable $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$ and a neighbour set $\mathcal{N}_i = \{j \in \mathcal{V} : (i,j) \in \mathcal{E}\}$.

**Step 2 — Write the cooperation objective (2 marks)**

The cooperation objective is a *separable-plus-coupling* function:

$$J(x) \;=\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i)}_{\text{separable (local) cost}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j)}_{\text{coupling (team) cost}}$$

The first term is local (each agent can compute it). The second term is the *cooperation objective* — it depends on multiple agents and represents what the team is collectively trying to achieve (e.g., consensus if $\phi_{ij} = a_{ij}\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$).

**Step 3 — State two cooperation constraints (2 marks)**

*Constraint 1 — Pair-wise collision avoidance:*
$$h_{ij}^{(1)}(x_i, x_j) \;=\; d_{safe} \;-\; \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \;\le\; 0,\quad \forall (i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$$

*Constraint 2 — Actuator / input saturation:*
$$h_i^{(2)}(u_i) \;=\; \Vert u_i\Vert \;-\; u_{\max} \;\le\; 0,\quad \forall i \in \mathcal{V}$$

**Step 4 — Write the complete constrained optimization problem (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x_1,\dots,x_N}\quad & \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & d_{safe} - \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \le 0,\quad \forall (i,j)\in\mathcal{E} \\
& \Vert u_i\Vert \le u_{\max},\quad \forall i \\
& x_i \text{ known only to } i \cup \mathcal{N}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The last line is the **distributed information constraint** — it forces the *algorithm* (not the formulation) to be distributed.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian (2 marks)**

Introduce multipliers $\mu_{ij} \ge 0$ for the collision constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(x, \mu) \;=\;& \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{(i,j)} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \\
&+\; \sum_{(i,j)} \mu_{ij}\bigl(d_{safe} - \Vert x_i - x_j\Vert\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose to agent-local sub-problems (2 marks)**

Collect all terms involving $x_i$:

$$\mathcal{L}_i(x_i) \;=\; f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}\Bigl[\phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \;+\; \mu_{ij}\bigl(d_{safe} - \Vert x_i - x_j\Vert\bigr)\Bigr]$$

Agent $i$ can compute this using only $x_i$ and $\{x_j\}_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}$.

**Step 3 — Take the gradient and write the update law (2 marks)**

$$\nabla_{x_i} \mathcal{L}_i \;=\; \nabla f_i(x_i) \;+\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}\bigl[\nabla_{x_i}\phi_{ij} \;-\; \mu_{ij}\,\tfrac{x_i - x_j}{\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert}\bigr]$$

Setting this to zero and using a gradient step of size $\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{\,x_i^{k+1} \;=\; x_i^k \;-\; \alpha\Bigl[\nabla f_i(x_i^k) \;+\; \sum_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}\bigl(\nabla_{x_i}\phi_{ij}(x_i^k, x_j^k) \;+\; \mu_{ij}^{k}\tfrac{x_i^k - x_j^k}{\Vert x_i^k - x_j^k\Vert}\bigr)\Bigr]\,}
$$

The multiplier $\mu_{ij}$ is updated by $\mu_{ij}^{k+1} = \bigl[\mu_{ij}^{k} + \eta\,(d_{safe} - \Vert x_i^k - x_j^k\Vert)\bigr]_+$, a projected gradient ascent (KKT sign condition $\mu_{ij} \ge 0$).

**Step 4 — Convergence condition (1 mark)**

The update converges to a KKT point of the global problem provided:
- Each $f_i$ is **strongly convex** with Lipschitz gradient,
- $\alpha$ satisfies $0 < \alpha < 2/L$,
- The communication graph $\mathcal{G}$ is **connected** ($\lambda_2(L) > 0$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain how formation control can be posed as a distributed cooperative optimization problem. Write the objective function and constraints explicitly. (7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the relation $J_c(x) = x^{\top} L x$ for the consensus cooperation objective and use it to analyse the convergence of three robots forming a triangle. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Pose the formation objective (2 marks)**

Let $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ be the position of robot $i$, and let $d_{ij}^{*}$ be the *desired* distance between robots $i$ and $j$. The cooperation objective is to keep actual inter-robot distances as close as possible to $d_{ij}^{*}$:

$$J_{\text{form}}(x) \;=\; \sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} a_{ij}\,\bigl\Vert \Vert x_i - x_j\Vert \;-\; d_{ij}^{*}\bigr\Vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Add cooperation constraints (2 marks)**

*Collision avoidance:*
$$\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \;\ge\; d_{safe} \;>\; 0$$

*Formation rigidity (for unique shape):*
The rigidity matrix $R(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{\vert\mathcal{E}\vert \times 2N}$ has rank $2N-3$ for a uniquely realizable triangular formation.

**Step 3 — Distributed information constraint (1 mark)**

Robot $i$ measures only $\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert$ for $j \in \mathcal{N}_i$ via onboard ranging sensors (UWB, cameras, LIDAR).

**Step 4 — Write the complete formulation (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{x_1,\dots,x_N}\quad & \sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\bigl(\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert - d_{ij}^{*}\bigr)^2 \\
\text{s.t.}\quad & \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert \ge d_{safe},\quad \forall (i,j) \\
& \text{rank}\bigl(R(x)\bigr) = 2N - 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the objective and constraints decompose over edges of $\mathcal{G}$, each robot can solve a *local* problem using only neighbor-range measurements — making the solution distributed.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the consensus cooperation objective (1 mark)**

$$J_c(x) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}} a_{ij}\,\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_c(x) \;=\;& \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\bigl(x_i^{\top}x_i + x_j^{\top}x_j - 2x_i^{\top}x_j\bigr) \\
=\;& \sum_{i=1}^{N} d_i\, x_i^{\top}x_i \;-\; \sum_{(i,j)} a_{ij}\, x_i^{\top}x_j
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Laplacian quadratic form (2 marks)**

With $L = D - A$:

$$\boxed{\,J_c(x) \;=\; x^{\top} L x\,}$$

> *Conversion logic*: The first term produces $x_i^{\top}x_i$ with coefficient $d_i = \sum_j a_{ij}$; the second subtracts $x_i^{\top}x_j$ with coefficient $a_{ij}$. Together these are exactly the entries of the symmetric matrix $L = D - A$.

**Step 4 — Three-robot triangle convergence analysis (2 marks)**

For three robots with unit weights, $L = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.

Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 0,\ \lambda_2 = \lambda_3 = 3$.

The zero eigenvalue corresponds to eigenvector $\mathbf{1} = (1,1,1)^{\top}$, meaning the team converges to a *common value* (the centroid $\bar{x} = \tfrac{1}{3}\sum_i x_i(0)$). The positive eigenvalue $\lambda_2 = 3$ gives the exponential decay rate of consensus error: $\Vert e(t)\Vert \le \Vert e(0)\Vert\, e^{-3t}$. Hence the triangle reaches consensus in finite time, which physically means all three robots meet at the team's average position.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cooperation Constraints and Objectives**

- [x] **Global problem structure**: $J = \sum_i f_i + f_c$, with $f_c$ being the *cooperation* (team-wide) part.
- [x] **Cooperation constraint** $h_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \le 0$ — couples agents through *edges* of $\mathcal{G}$.
- [x] **Information constraint** — each agent only sees $x_i$ and $\{x_j\}_{j\in\mathcal{N}_i}$; this is what makes the algorithm *distributed*.
- [x] **Consensus objective** reduces to the Laplacian quadratic form $J_c(x) = x^{\top} L x$.
- [x] **Graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$ has eigenvalues $0 = \lambda_1 \le \lambda_2 \le \dots \le \lambda_N$; $\lambda_2 > 0 \Leftrightarrow$ graph connected.
- [x] **Distributed update law** combines local gradient + consensus coupling:

$$x_i^{k+1} = x_i^k - \alpha\nabla f_i(x_i^k) - \beta\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i}(x_i^k - x_j^k)$$

- [x] **Convergence needs**: (i) strongly convex $f_i$, (ii) step-size $0 < \alpha < 2/L$, (iii) connected $\mathcal{G}$.
- [x] **Lagrangian + dual decomposition** is the *bridge* from a centralized problem to a distributed algorithm.
- [x] **KKT sign condition**: multipliers for inequality cooperation constraints must satisfy $\mu_{ij} \ge 0$.
- [x] **Engineering pay-off**: optimization gives provable optimality, constraint handling, and scalability — three reasons KTU examiners love this formulation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip**: Whenever you see "distributed cooperative control with optimization", write the four-line answer:
> 1. The objective $J = \sum_i f_i + f_c$,
> 2. The cooperation constraints $h_{ij} \le 0$,
> 3. The information structure (neighbors only),
> 4. The distributed update law and convergence condition.
>
> These four lines cover roughly 80 % of marks in both Part A and Part B on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordination Variables and Coordination Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coordination Variables and Coordination Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In cooperative (multi-robot) robotics, a **Coordination Variable** is a scalar or vector quantity that characterizes the **relative geometric or kinematic state** between two or more robots in a formation. These variables form the *minimal mathematical description* of how a group of robots is geometrically arranged at any given instant.

A **Coordination Function**, on the other hand, is a **prescribed mathematical mapping** (typically a function of time, configuration, or task state) that specifies the **desired value, evolution, or constraint** imposed upon one or more coordination variables. It is the *control objective* expressed mathematically.

In the formal framework of cooperative control (as introduced by Murray, Pallottino, and consensus-based literature), a multi-robot system with $N$ robots has individual configurations $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^n$. The collective configuration is $q = [q_1^T, q_2^T, \ldots, q_N^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{nN}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Murray Framework):**
> - **Coordination Variable:** $\alpha = h(q) = h(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_N)$ — a function of the collective configuration.
> - **Coordination Function:** $\alpha_d(t)$ — the prescribed time-varying desired value of the coordination variable.
> - The **coordination problem** reduces to designing a controller such that $\alpha(t) \to \alpha_d(t)$ as $t \to \infty$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **formation marching band** performing at a parade.

- The **musicians (robots)** are the individual agents.
- The **coordination variable** is the *distance between any two adjacent musicians* — it is what we observe to describe the formation.
- The **coordination function** is the **choreographer's instruction** — "during the bridge, the spacing between row 1 and row 2 should be exactly 2 meters; during the finale, it should be 1.5 meters."

The musicians do not know about the global pattern — they only need to sense and maintain the **relative spacing** (coordination variable) as dictated by the **choreography** (coordination function).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> - *Coordination Variable* = WHAT we measure between robots (the gap, angle, distance).
> - *Coordination Function* = WHAT we want that measurement to be (the prescribed target).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Number of robots | $N$ | $2 \le N \le 100$ (typical) |
| Coordination variable dimension | $m$ | Depends on task (1 for distance, 2 for bearing, etc.) |
| Graph connectivity | $\lambda_2(L)$ | Fiedler eigenvalue (algebraic connectivity) |
| Reference frame | $\mathcal{F}_W, \mathcal{F}_B$ | World / Body frames |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Coordination Variables

Coordination variables are classified by their **physical nature**:

1. **Geometric / Position-Based**
   - Relative position: $p_{ij} = p_j - p_i$
   - Distance: $d_{ij} = \vert\vert p_j - p_i \vert\vert$
   - Bearing: $\beta_{ij} = \text{atan2}(p_j - p_i)$
   - Area, perimeter, centroid of a formation

2. **Velocity-Based**
   - Relative velocity: $v_{ij} = v_j - v_i$
   - Velocity alignment (consensus)

3. **Higher-Order (Acceleration, Force)**
   - Internal force, impedance, contact forces in co-manipulation.

4. **Task-Space / Functional**
   - End-effector relative pose, payload orientation.

## 2.2 Classification of Coordination Functions

A coordination function $\alpha_d(t)$ can take several canonical forms:

| Type | Mathematical Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant (Stationary)** | $\alpha_d(t) = c$ | Static formation |
| **Trajectory (Time-Varying)** | $\alpha_d(t) = f(t)$ | Moving formation / reconfiguration |
| **State-Dependent** | $\alpha_d(t) = g(x)$ | Obstacle avoidance, reactive |
| **Consensus** | $\alpha_d = \text{avg}(\alpha_i)$ | Flocking, rendezvous |
| **Constraint** | $\alpha_d \in \mathcal{C}$ | Constrained formations |

## 2.3 Operational Logic — The Coordination Loop

1. **Sense**: Each robot measures its own state $q_i$ and (via communication) neighbors' states $q_j$.
2. **Compute**: Form coordination variable $\alpha = h(q_1, \ldots, q_N)$.
3. **Compare**: Compute error $e = \alpha_d(t) - \alpha(t)$.
4. **Act**: Generate control input $u_i = K \cdot e$ to drive $e \to 0$.
5. **Communicate**: Broadcast $q_i$ to neighbors (graph $\mathcal{G}$).

## 2.4 The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHY coordination variables?**
> Because a multi-robot system with $N$ robots and $n$ DOF each has $nN$ total degrees of freedom — a hopeless control problem. Coordination variables **reduce** this dimensionality by focusing only on the *task-relevant* relative quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOW are coordination functions chosen?**
> They must be **feasible** (achievable by the actuators), **connected** (the graph must allow information flow), and **collision-free** (inter-robot distances must remain positive).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Collective configuration | $q = [q_1^T, q_2^T, \ldots, q_N^T]^T$ | $\mathbb{R}^{nN}$ |
| 2 | Coordination variable | $\alpha = h(q)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 3 | Coordination function | $\alpha_d(t)$ | $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 4 | Tracking error | $e(t) = \alpha_d(t) - \alpha(t)$ | Drives the control law |
| 5 | Linear control input | $u_i = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | $K_p, K_d$ are gain matrices |
| 6 | Relative distance | $d_{ij} = \vert\vert p_j - p_i \vert\vert_2$ | Scalar (meters) |
| 7 | Bearing angle | $\theta_{ij} = \text{atan2}(p_{jy} - p_{iy}, p_{jx} - p_{ix})$ | Radians |
| 8 | Centroid (formation ref.) | $c = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i$ | $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ or $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ |
| 9 | Formation area | $A = \frac{1}{2}\left\vert\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i)\right\vert$ | $m^2$ |
| 10 | Laplacian (graph) | $L = D - A$ | $L \mathbf{1} = 0$ |
| 11 | Consensus dynamics | $\dot{x}_i = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_i - x_j)$ | $\alpha_d = \text{avg}(x)$ |
| 12 | Stability condition | $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ (connected graph) | Fiedler value |

> **Engineer's Field Note:** These formulas are used in **warehouse robotics** (Amazon Kiva), **agricultural swarms**, **search-and-rescue UAVs**, and **autonomous highway platoons** (truck platooning by Peloton, Scania).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Raw State to Coordination Variable

Consider $N$ mobile robots in $\mathbb{R}^2$, with positions $p_i = [x_i, y_i]^T$. We derive a canonical coordination variable — the **relative distance** — and the corresponding coordination function.

### Step 1: Define Individual Robot Positions

$$p_i(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x_i(t) \\ y_i(t) \end{bmatrix}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

Each robot has dynamics (unicycle or point-mass) governed by $\dot{p}_i = u_i$, where $u_i$ is the velocity control input.

### Step 2: Form the Pairwise Relative Position

For a pair $(i, j)$ where $i \neq j$, the relative position vector is:

$$p_{ij} = p_j - p_i = \begin{bmatrix} x_j - x_i \\ y_j - y_i \end{bmatrix}$$

**Explanation:** This vector points from robot $i$ to robot $j$. Its magnitude and direction encode the geometric relationship.

### Step 3: Compute the Scalar Distance Coordination Variable

The **distance** coordination variable is:

$$\alpha_{ij} = d_{ij} = \vert\vert p_{ij} \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{(x_j - x_i)^2 + (y_j - y_i)^2}$$

**Explanation:** The Euclidean norm collapses the 2D vector into a single scalar — a *minimal* description of the inter-robot spacing. This is the most widely used coordination variable in formation control.

### Step 4: Differentiate to Obtain the Rate of Change

Using the chain rule:

$$\dot{\alpha}_{ij} = \frac{d}{dt}\vert\vert p_{ij} \vert\vert_2 = \frac{p_{ij}^T \dot{p}_{ij}}{\vert\vert p_{ij} \vert\vert_2}$$

Substituting $\dot{p}_{ij} = \dot{p}_j - \dot{p}_i = u_j - u_i$:

$$\dot{\alpha}_{ij} = \frac{p_{ij}^T (u_j - u_i)}{\alpha_{ij}}$$

**Explanation:** This gives the rate of change of the distance in terms of the robots' velocity inputs. The numerator is the dot product of the line-of-sight vector with the relative velocity.

### Step 5: Define the Coordination Function

Suppose the desired inter-robot distance is $d^* > 0$ (constant formation). The coordination function is:

$$\alpha_d(t) = d^* \quad (\text{stationary case})$$

For a **time-varying** formation (e.g., a contractile/expanding formation):

$$\alpha_d(t) = d_0 + A \sin(\omega t)$$

where $A$ is amplitude and $\omega$ is the oscillation frequency.

### Step 6: Form the Tracking Error

$$e_{ij}(t) = \alpha_d(t) - \alpha_{ij}(t)$$

**Explanation:** The control objective is $e_{ij}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

### Step 7: Design a Proportional Control Law

A simple choice along the line-of-sight direction:

$$u_{ij}^{\text{ctrl}} = k_p \cdot e_{ij} \cdot \frac{p_{ij}}{\alpha_{ij}}, \quad k_p > 0$$

The full control input for robot $i$ is summed over all its neighbors $j \in \mathcal{N}_i$:

$$u_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} k_p (\alpha_d - \alpha_{ij}) \frac{p_{ij}}{\alpha_{ij}}$$

### Step 8: Lyapunov Stability Argument

Define the candidate Lyapunov function:

$$V = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij}^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} (\alpha_d - \alpha_{ij})^2$$

This is positive semi-definite ($V \ge 0$) and zero only when all distances match. Differentiating:

$$\dot{V} = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij} \cdot (-\dot{\alpha}_{ij}) = -\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij} \cdot \frac{p_{ij}^T (u_j - u_i)}{\alpha_{ij}}$$

With the control law in Step 7 and assuming symmetric information flow, one can show $\dot{V} \le 0$, which proves **asymptotic stability** of the desired formation.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Derivations

| Step | Output | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_i \in \mathbb{R}^2$ | Individual state |
| 2 | $p_{ij} = p_j - p_i$ | Pairwise geometry |
| 3 | $\alpha_{ij} = \vert\vert p_{ij} \vert\vert$ | Coordination variable |
| 4 | $\dot{\alpha}_{ij}$ | Sensitivity w.r.t. inputs |
| 5 | $\alpha_d(t)$ | Coordination function |
| 6 | $e_{ij} = \alpha_d - \alpha_{ij}$ | Tracking error |
| 7 | $u_i$ control law | Drives $e \to 0$ |
| 8 | $V$ and $\dot{V}$ | Stability proof |

> **Engineering Insight:** The derivation above is the *core* of every formation control algorithm used in industry — from **UAV light shows** (Verity Studios, Intel) to **AUV ocean surveys**. The choice of coordination variable (distance vs. bearing) determines the sensor requirement (range vs. vision).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Coordination Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification alpha_d of t] --> B[Reference Generator]
    B --> C[Desired Coordination Function]
    D[Robot 1 State q1] --> E[Sensor Module]
    F[Robot 2 State qN] --> E
    G[Robot N State qN] --> E
    E --> H[Compute Coordination Variable alpha h q]
    H --> I[Error Computation e alpha d minus alpha]
    C --> I
    I --> J[Controller Kp Kd]
    J --> K[Control Input u i]
    K --> L[Robot Actuators]
    L --> M[Updated Robot States]
    M --> D
    M --> F
    M --> G
    H --> N[Communication Graph Laplacian L]
    N --> J
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy: Coordination Variables vs. Coordination Functions

```mermaid
graph TD
    CV[Coordination Variables alpha] --> CV1[Relative Position p_ij]
    CV --> CV2[Distance d_ij]
    CV --> CV3[Bearing theta_ij]
    CV --> CV4[Velocity Consensus v_i]
    CV --> CV5[Centroid c]
    CV1 --> EX1[Used in leader follower]
    CV2 --> EX2[Used in formation control]
    CV3 --> EX3[Used in bearing only]
    CF[Coordination Functions alpha_d] --> CF1[Constant c]
    CF --> CF2[Trajectory f of t]
    CF --> CF3[State dependent g of x]
    CF1 --> EX1
    CF2 --> EX4[Moving formations]
    CF3 --> EX5[Reactive obstacle avoidance]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Multi-Robot Coordination System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Perception_Layer
    S1[Robot 1 Sensors]
    S2[Robot N Sensors]
    end
    subgraph Communication_Layer
    WSN[Wireless Network]
    GR[Graph Topology Laplacian L]
    end
    subgraph Computation_Layer
    CV_Module[Coordination Variable Module h of q]
    CF_Module[Coordination Function Module alpha d of t]
    ERR[Error e]
    CTRL[Controller]
    end
    subgraph Actuation_Layer
    A1[Robot 1 Actuators]
    AN[Robot N Actuators]
    end
    S1 --> CV_Module
    S2 --> CV_Module
    WSN --> GR
    GR --> CTRL
    CF_Module --> ERR
    CV_Module --> ERR
    ERR --> CTRL
    CTRL --> A1
    CTRL --> AN
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing a Coordination Variable

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Select Coordination Variable] --> Q1{What sensors are available?}
    Q1 -->|Range sensors LiDAR, UWB| R1[Use Distance d_ij]
    Q1 -->|Vision Camera| R2[Use Bearing theta_ij]
    Q1 -->|GPS and IMU| R3[Use Relative Position p_ij]
    Q1 -->|Velocity encoders| R4[Use Velocity Consensus v_i]
    R1 --> Q2{Is formation rigid?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R5[Distance based formation control]
    Q2 -->|No| R6[Bearing based formation control]
    R2 --> R6
    R3 --> R7[Position based consensus]
    R4 --> R8[Velocity flocking]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define coordination variable and coordination function in a multi-robot system. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **coordination variable** $\alpha = h(q)$ is a function of the collective configuration $q = [q_1^T, \ldots, q_N^T]^T$ of a multi-robot system, describing the *relative geometric state* between robots. **Example:** The distance $d_{ij} = \vert\vert p_j - p_i \vert\vert$ between two robots.

A **coordination function** $\alpha_d(t)$ is the *prescribed time-varying desired value* of the coordination variable. **Example:** A constant desired spacing $d^* = 1.5$ m for a convoy of autonomous vehicles.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**Why is the choice of coordination variable important in cooperative robotics?**

**Model Answer:**
The choice of coordination variable is important because it determines:
1. The **sensor requirements** (distance needs range sensors; bearing needs vision).
2. The **dimensionality** of the control problem — only task-relevant variables are controlled.
3. The **stability and feasibility** of the formation (e.g., distance-based control is robust to GPS loss).
4. The **information flow** required via the communication graph.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **distance coordination variable** between two robots and its time-derivative. Show how it is used to formulate a coordination function for a **constant-formation** problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3-robot triangular formation, write the coordination variables and coordination functions, and design a **proportional control law** to maintain a constant side length $L$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Position of robot $i$:** $p_i = [x_i, y_i]^T$.

**Step 2: Relative position:** $p_{ij} = p_j - p_i$.

**Step 3: Distance coordination variable:**
$$\alpha_{ij} = \vert\vert p_j - p_i \vert\vert_2 = \sqrt{(x_j - x_i)^2 + (y_j - y_i)^2}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate:**
$$\dot{\alpha}_{ij} = \frac{p_{ij}^T (\dot{p}_j - \dot{p}_i)}{\alpha_{ij}} = \frac{p_{ij}^T (u_j - u_i)}{\alpha_{ij}}$$

**Step 5: Coordination function (constant):** $\alpha_d(t) = L$ (desired spacing).

**Step 6: Error:** $e_{ij} = L - \alpha_{ij}$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

For a triangle with robots 1, 2, 3, the three coordination variables are:
$$\alpha_{12} = \vert\vert p_2 - p_1 \vert\vert, \quad \alpha_{23} = \vert\vert p_3 - p_2 \vert\vert, \quad \alpha_{31} = \vert\vert p_1 - p_3 \vert\vert$$

Coordination functions: $\alpha_d^{(12)} = \alpha_d^{(23)} = \alpha_d^{(31)} = L$.

Control law for robot $i$ (summing contributions from all neighbors):
$$u_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} k_p (L - \alpha_{ij}) \frac{p_{ij}}{\alpha_{ij}}, \quad k_p > 0$$

For example, for robot 1 with neighbors 2 and 3:
$$u_1 = k_p (L - \alpha_{12}) \frac{p_{12}}{\alpha_{12}} + k_p (L - \alpha_{13}) \frac{p_{13}}{\alpha_{13}}$$

This drives each side of the triangle to length $L$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a suitable diagram the concept of **coordination variables and coordination functions** using a 2-robot leader-follower formation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lyapunov stability condition** for a distance-based formation with $N$ robots, and state the role of the **graph Laplacian** in ensuring coordination. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Leader-follower setup:** Let robot 1 (leader) move with velocity $v_L$, and robot 2 (follower) must maintain a desired offset $\Delta = [\Delta_x, \Delta_y]^T$.

**Coordination variable:** The relative position of the follower w.r.t. the leader.
$$\alpha = p_2 - p_1 = [x_2 - x_1, \, y_2 - y_1]^T$$

**Coordination function (time-varying):** The desired offset.
$$\alpha_d(t) = \Delta = [\Delta_x, \Delta_y]^T$$

**Tracking error:** $e = \alpha_d - \alpha$.

**Control law (follower):** $u_2 = \dot{\alpha}_d + K_p e = K_p (\Delta - (p_2 - p_1))$, ensuring $\alpha \to \alpha_d$ as $t \to \infty$.

*Diagram:* A 2D plane showing the leader at $p_1$ moving along $v_L$, the follower at $p_2$, and a dashed vector $\Delta$ connecting them.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Lyapunov function:**
$$V = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} (\alpha_d - \alpha_{ij})^2 = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij}^2$$

This is positive semi-definite.

**Derivative:**
$$\dot{V} = \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij} (-\dot{\alpha}_{ij})$$

Substituting $\dot{\alpha}_{ij} = \frac{p_{ij}^T (u_j - u_i)}{\alpha_{ij}}$ and the control law $u_i = \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} k_p e_{ij} \frac{p_{ij}}{\alpha_{ij}}$:

One can show (with the symmetric graph assumption and identical dynamics):
$$\dot{V} = -k_p \sum_{(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}} e_{ij}^2 \le 0$$

**Stability condition:** The graph Laplacian $L = D - A$ must be **positive semi-definite** with $\lambda_2(L) > 0$, i.e., the communication graph must be **connected**. This ensures information propagates and consensus is reachable.

**Role of Laplacian:** $L$ encodes *who can talk to whom*. A connected graph ($\lambda_2 > 0$) is **necessary and sufficient** for the formation to converge.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing the symbols:** Many students write $\alpha = h(q)$ as the *function* and $\alpha_d(t)$ as the *variable*. **It is the opposite** — $\alpha$ is the *measured* variable, $\alpha_d$ is the *desired* function. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the dot product step** in deriving $\dot{\alpha}_{ij}$ — directly jumping to the control law. The chain rule step is mandatory. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Missing units / physical interpretation** — every formula must end with a *real-world* remark. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Not stating the graph connectivity condition** $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ — this is a KTU favorite. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Sign error in the control law:** The control must push *toward* the neighbor when $e > 0$, i.e., $u_i$ proportional to $+p_{ij}$, not $-p_{ij}$. (-1 mark)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coordination Variable** $\alpha = h(q)$: a *measured* function of the collective state. Examples: distance $d_{ij}$, bearing $\theta_{ij}$, relative velocity.
- **Coordination Function** $\alpha_d(t)$: a *prescribed* desired trajectory. Can be constant, time-varying, or state-dependent.
- **Tracking error** $e = \alpha_d - \alpha$ drives the controller.
- **Reduction of dimensionality:** From $nN$ raw DOF down to $m$ task-relevant DOF.
- **Graph Laplacian** $L = D - A$ governs information flow; $\lambda_2(L) > 0$ is needed for convergence.
- **Standard derivation chain:** $p_i \to p_{ij} \to \alpha_{ij} = \vert\vert p_{ij} \vert\vert \to \dot{\alpha}_{ij} \to e_{ij} \to u_i$.
- **Lyapunov function** $V = \frac{1}{2}\sum e_{ij}^2$ is the standard proof tool — must show $\dot{V} \le 0$.
- **Sensors dictate the choice:** LiDAR → distance; Camera → bearing; GPS → position; Encoders → velocity.
- **Industrial applications:** warehouse fleets, truck platooning, UAV swarms, AUV surveys, satellite formation flying.
- **KTU-typical marks split:** Part A (3 marks) = definition + 1 example; Part B (14 marks) = (a) derivation 7 marks + (b) design/analysis 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centralized Cooperation Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT861 - CO-OPERATIVE ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Centralized Cooperation Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Centralized Cooperation Scheme** in cooperative robotics is a multi-robot coordination architecture in which a **single, computationally powerful central planner (master node)** possesses complete global knowledge of the environment, task requirements, and the state of every robot (slave/agent) in the team. The central node is solely responsible for **task allocation, motion planning, trajectory generation, and conflict resolution**, while individual robots execute the high-level commands received from the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> In a Centralized Cooperation Scheme, all decision-making, optimization, and coordination logic resides in a single central processor. Robots act purely as actuated agents that report their sensor data back to the center and follow received motion/actuation commands in a master–slave communication topology.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine a busy international airport. Hundreds of aircraft (robots) are on the tarmac and in the sky. A single **Air Traffic Control (ATC) Tower** sits at the center and:

| ATC Tower Role | Centralized Robot Scheme Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sees the radar of **all** aircraft | Central planner has **global state vector** of all robots |
| Tells each pilot **when to take off, route, altitude** | Central planner issues **trajectory commands** to each robot |
| Pilots obey and report back position | Robots execute commands and **broadcast pose/sensor data** |
| If the tower shuts down → chaos | If central node fails → **complete system collapse** |

This "**tower-and-aircraft**" mental model is the easiest way for a KTU student to internalize the architecture, its strength (global optimality), and its fatal weakness (single point of failure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU exams)**
> - Communication topology: **Star (Centralized)**
> - Decision-making node count: **1 (single master)**
> - Information availability at master: **Full / Global**
> - Typical planning complexity: **O(n²) to O(n³)** in number of robots *n*
> - Scalability: **Poor beyond ~10–15 robots** (computation & bandwidth bottleneck)
> - Optimality: **Globally optimal** (when problem is tractable)

### 1.3 Why Study Centralized Schemes?

Despite their well-known drawbacks, centralized schemes remain a **cornerstone of KTU Module 3** because:

1. They form the **baseline architecture** against which distributed and hybrid schemes are compared.
2. Many industrial implementations (factory AMRs, warehouse fleets, AGV systems) **still use centralized planners** in practice.
3. They introduce students to **classical assignment (Hungarian Algorithm)** and **centralized trajectory optimization** — both frequently asked in KTU exams.
4. They build the foundation for understanding **formation control with a virtual leader** and **leader-follower schemes** studied later in cooperative robotics.

### 1.4 Architectural Snapshot

The centralized cooperation scheme can be decomposed into **four functional layers**, all of which physically/logically reside in (or are orchestrated by) the central processor:

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Functional Layers**
> 1. **Perception & State Estimation Layer** — Fuses incoming sensor data from all robots into a single global world model.
> 2. **Task Allocation Layer** — Assigns sub-tasks (pick, place, transport, patrol, cover) to individual robots.
> 3. **Motion Planning Layer** — Computes collision-free trajectories in a unified configuration space.
> 4. **Execution & Monitoring Layer** — Streams commands to robots and verifies compliance in real time.

These layers will be revisited in **SECTION 4 (Diagrams)** and are essential for solving 14-mark questions in **SECTION 5**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

A centralized cooperation scheme operates as a continuous **Sense → Plan → Act → Broadcast** loop. Each iteration of the loop is anchored at the central node.

1. **Sense (Slave → Master):** Every robot *i* broadcasts its current pose $q_i \in \mathbb{R}^{n_i}$, velocity $\dot{q}_i$, sensor readings $s_i$, and task status $T_i^{status}$ to the central planner over a wireless channel.
2. **Plan (Master Internal):** The central planner aggregates all $(q_i, \dot{q}_i, s_i, T_i^{status})$ into a **global state vector**:
$$ X(t) = [q_1^T, q_2^T, \ldots, q_n^T, \dot{q}_1^T, \dot{q}_2^T, \ldots, \dot{q}_n^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{N} $$
where $N = 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$ is the total configuration dimension of the multi-robot system.

3. **Task Allocation:** Using global information, the central planner solves an assignment problem (Section 2.3) to bind each sub-task to a specific robot.
4. **Motion Planning:** Using the global configuration space $\mathcal{C} = \mathcal{C}_1 \times \mathcal{C}_2 \times \ldots \times \mathcal{C}_n$, the planner computes a sequence of collision-free configurations from $X(t)$ to the goal state $X_{goal}$.
5. **Act (Master → Slave):** The planner broadcasts low-level velocity/position setpoints $\dot{q}_i^{ref}$ (or $q_i^{ref}$) to each robot at a fixed control rate.
6. **Monitor & Repeat:** Robots track the setpoints; any deviation is fed back in the next Sense phase.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Centralized Architecture

The centralization is mathematically motivated by the desire to solve **one large optimization problem** rather than *n* coupled smaller problems. For a cooperative task with cost $J_i$ for each robot and coupling cost $J_{ij}$ between robot pairs:

$$ J_{total}^{centralized} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} J_i(q_i, u_i) + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=i+1}^{n} J_{ij}(q_i, q_j, u_i, u_j) $$

In a centralized scheme, this **single global cost is minimized in one shot** at the master node. In a distributed scheme, each robot estimates only its local terms and iteratively reaches consensus — which is mathematically harder and yields only locally optimal (or Nash equilibrium) solutions.

### 2.3 Task Allocation — Hungarian Algorithm (Centralized)

For a discrete assignment problem with *n* robots and *n* tasks, the **Hungarian Algorithm** solves the optimal assignment in **O(n³)** time. The central planner constructs an $n \times n$ cost matrix $C$ where $c_{ij}$ is the cost of assigning robot *i* to task *j*, then solves:

$$ \min_{\Pi} \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i,\Pi(i)} $$

subject to $\Pi$ being a permutation (one-to-one assignment). The result is the **optimal global assignment** that minimizes total execution cost.

### 2.4 Centralized Path Planning

The central planner operates in the **composite configuration space** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \{X \mid X \text{ collision-free for all robots}\}$. Common centralized planners include:

- **Centralized A\*** (or **A\* on composite graph**)
- **Centralized RRT / RRT\***
- **Priority-based planning** with serialization at the central node
- **Optimal control / MPC** formulations for dynamic obstacles

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for KTU Module 3 centralized-scheme numerical and theory questions. Memorize the variable definitions, the boundary conditions, and the units.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X(t) = [q_1^T, q_2^T, \ldots, q_n^T, \dot{q}_1^T, \ldots, \dot{q}_n^T]^T$ | **Global state vector** stacking all robot poses and velocities | Dimensionless (rad or m) |
| 2 | $J_{total}^{centralized} = \sum_i J_i + \sum_i \sum_{j>i} J_{ij}$ | **Centralized global cost** = individual costs + pairwise coupling costs | Energy / Time / Distance |
| 3 | $\min_\Pi \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_{i,\Pi(i)}$ | **Hungarian assignment objective** — $\Pi$ is a permutation of {1..n} | Currency / Time / m |
| 4 | Hungarian complexity: $O(n^3)$ | **Time complexity** of the Hungarian algorithm | Floating-point ops |
| 5 | $J^* = \arg\min_{X \in \mathcal{C}_{free}} J_{total}(X)$ | **Centralized motion planning objective** | Cost value |
| 6 | $\dot{q}_i^{ref} = f_i(X, X_{goal})$ | **Reference velocity** issued by central planner to robot *i* | m/s or rad/s |
| 7 | Bandwidth requirement: $B \geq n \cdot f_s \cdot (b_{pose} + b_{sensor})$ | **Minimum uplink bandwidth** for *n* robots at sampling freq. $f_s$ | bits/s (Hz × bits) |
| 8 | Planning latency bound: $T_{plan} \leq T_{ctrl}$ | Central planner must finish within one control period $T_{ctrl}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Communication delay: $\tau_{comm}$ | One-way propagation + transmission delay from robot *i* to master | seconds |
| 10 | Single point of failure (SPOF) | Failure of central node ⇒ **100% system halt** | Boolean (fail/safe) |
| 11 | Scalability limit: $n \leq n_{max} \approx 10\text{–}15$ | Practical robot count beyond which computation/bandwidth fails | robots |
| 12 | Optimality guarantee | **Globally optimal** (centralized) vs **Nash / local optimum** (distributed) | — |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Centralized Schemes are Deployed Today**
> - **Amazon Kiva / Kestrel robots** in fulfillment centers (central fleet manager).
> - **Automated container terminals** (Rotterdam Maasvlakte II — central AGV dispatcher).
> - **Surgical robotics master–slave systems** (da Vinci surgical console).
> - **Military coordinated UAV swarms in pre-2020 demonstrations** (typically centralized ground control).
> - **Smart factory AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** with a fleet orchestrator.

In each of these systems, the **central planner runs on a high-performance server or edge-cloud node**, and the robots act as mobile actuators receiving streaming commands. This is the textbook definition of a centralized cooperation scheme.

### 2.7 Advantages and Disadvantages (Must-Know for KTU)

**Advantages:**
- **Global optimality** of task allocation and motion plans.
- **Simple to implement** — one decision-maker, one world model.
- **Conflict-free trajectories** guaranteed by construction.
- **Centralized fault diagnosis** — easy to monitor and re-plan.

**Disadvantages:**
- **Single point of failure (SPOF)** — master crash ⇒ system collapse.
- **Poor scalability** — $O(n^3)$ planning, $O(n)$ communication.
- **Communication bottleneck** — bandwidth grows linearly with *n*.
- **Latency-sensitive** — long $\tau_{comm}$ degrades real-time control.
- **No autonomy** for individual robots if link drops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Centralized Task Allocation via Hungarian Algorithm

We will derive, in complete detail, the assignment cost matrix construction and the optimal permutation $\Pi^*$ for a small cooperative scenario commonly appearing in KTU exams.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider **n = 3** mobile robots $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and **n = 3** inspection tasks $T_1, T_2, T_3$. The central planner estimates the **travel time** (in seconds) for each robot–task pair based on current pose and known task locations:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix}
c_{11} & c_{12} & c_{13} \\
c_{21} & c_{22} & c_{23} \\
c_{31} & c_{32} & c_{33}
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
8 & 4 & 7 \\
5 & 2 & 3 \\
9 & 4 & 6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The centralized objective is:

$$
J_{assign} = \min_{\Pi} \sum_{i=1}^{3} c_{i,\Pi(i)}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Hungarian Algorithm Execution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Do not skip transitions when answering in the exam.**

**Step 1 — Row Reduction.** Subtract the row minimum from each row.

$$
\text{Row minima: } \min(8,4,7)=4, \ \min(5,2,3)=2, \ \min(9,4,6)=4
$$

$$
C^{(1)} = \begin{bmatrix}
8-4 & 4-4 & 7-4 \\
5-2 & 2-2 & 3-2 \\
9-4 & 4-4 & 6-4
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
4 & 0 & 3 \\
3 & 0 & 1 \\
5 & 0 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Column Reduction.** Subtract the column minimum from each column.

$$
\text{Column minima: } \min(4,3,5)=3, \ \min(0,0,0)=0, \ \min(3,1,2)=1
$$

$$
C^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
4-3 & 0-0 & 3-1 \\
3-3 & 0-0 & 1-1 \\
5-3 & 0-0 & 2-1
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 2 \\
0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Cover all zeros with minimum number of lines.**

Looking at $C^{(2)}$, we can cover **all three zeros** in column 2 with a single vertical line:

$$
C^{(2)} = \begin{bmatrix}
1 & \cancel{0} & 2 \\
0 & \cancel{0} & 0 \\
2 & \cancel{0} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Number of covering lines = **1**. Since number of lines $<$ matrix order (3), we proceed to Step 4.

**Step 4 — Create additional zeros.** Find the **minimum uncovered element** (uncovered cells are $C_{11}=1$, $C_{13}=2$, $C_{21}=0$, $C_{23}=0$, $C_{31}=2$, $C_{33}=1$):

$$
m = \min(1, 2, 0, 0, 2, 1) = 0
$$

Since the minimum uncovered element is already 0, no subtraction is needed in this step. We instead **shift to marking zeros** for assignment.

**Step 5 — Assign zeros (Greedy on zeros).**

Since multiple zeros exist (positions $(2,1), (2,2), (2,3)$ plus $(1,2), (3,2)$), we apply the standard **marking procedure** of the Hungarian method. Mark row 2 first (it has the most zeros, three zeros). Then column 1 has a marked zero, so we mark column 1. Row 1 has a zero in column 2 — but column 2 is unmarked, so no further marking.

Final marked zeros give the **optimal assignment**:
- $R_2 \rightarrow T_1$ (cost = 5)
- $R_1 \rightarrow T_2$ (cost = 4)
- $R_3 \rightarrow T_3$ (cost = 6)

#### 3.1.3 Optimal Cost

$$
J_{assign}^* = c_{21} + c_{12} + c_{33} = 5 + 4 + 6 = 15 \text{ seconds}
$$

The permutation is $\Pi^* = \{1 \to 2,\ 2 \to 1,\ 3 \to 3\}$.

#### 3.1.4 Verification

Let us verify by checking all 6 permutations ($3! = 6$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pi_1 &: (1,2,3) \Rightarrow 8+2+6 = 16 \\
\Pi_2 &: (1,3,2) \Rightarrow 8+3+4 = 15 \\
\Pi_3 &: (2,1,3) \Rightarrow 4+5+6 = \mathbf{15} \quad \leftarrow \text{optimal} \\
\Pi_4 &: (2,3,1) \Rightarrow 4+3+9 = 16 \\
\Pi_5 &: (3,1,2) \Rightarrow 7+5+4 = 16 \\
\Pi_6 &: (3,2,1) \Rightarrow 7+2+9 = 18
\end{aligned}
$$

Both $\Pi_2$ and $\Pi_3$ yield the **global minimum cost of 15 seconds**. The Hungarian algorithm returns one such optimal solution.

### 3.2 Derivation: Centralized Path Planning Cost Bound

For a team of *n* robots in the composite configuration space, the central planner solves:

$$
X^*(t) = \arg\min_{X(t) \in \mathcal{C}_{free}} \int_{0}^{T} \Big[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \| \dot{q}_i(t) \|^2_{R_i} + \sum_{i<j} \phi_{ij}(\| q_i - q_j \|) \Big] dt
$$

where:
- $R_i \succ 0$ is a positive-definite control weight matrix for robot *i*.
- $\phi_{ij}$ is a **pairwise collision-avoidance penalty** that grows large when robots *i* and *j* come too close.

A common choice is:

$$
\phi_{ij}(d) = \begin{cases}
K \cdot \left( \dfrac{1}{d^2} - \dfrac{1}{d_{safe}^2} \right), & d < d_{safe} \\
0, & d \geq d_{safe}
\end{cases}
$$
